Test IV: Order Invariance Details
Three class orderings (original, reversed, shuffled) are compared per H&K. Pass criterion: max pairwise change rate < 5%.


Show code
iv <- s1$test_iv

cat(sprintf("Reversed change rate: %.1f%% (%d/%d)\n",
            iv$change_rate_reversed * 100, iv$n_changed_reversed, iv$n_total))


Reversed change rate: 0.0% (0/10)


Show code
cat(sprintf("Shuffled change rate: %.1f%% (%d/%d)\n",
            iv$change_rate_shuffled * 100, iv$n_changed_shuffled, iv$n_total))


Shuffled change rate: 0.0% (0/10)


Show code
cat(sprintf("Fleiss's kappa: %.3f (%s)\n", iv$fleiss_kappa, iv$kappa_interpretation))


Fleiss's kappa: 1.000 (Near-perfect agreement)


Show code
if (!iv$pass) {
  cat("\nOrder invariance test FAILED. Changed predictions:\n\n")
  iv$details |>
    filter(changed_reversed | changed_shuffled) |>
    select(text_id, label_original, label_reversed, label_shuffled,
           changed_reversed, changed_shuffled) |>
    gt() |>
    gt_theme_report()
} else {
  cat(sprintf("\nOrder invariance PASSED (max change rate: %.1f%%, threshold: <5%%).\n",
              iv$change_rate * 100))
}



Order invariance PASSED (max change rate: 0.0%, threshold: <5%).

title: C1: Measure Identification
C1: Measure Identification
C1: Measure Identification
text:
Iteration Notes
Record codebook revision decisions and their rationale here after reviewing S2 and S3 results.

v0.1.0: Initial codebook. Review results above before deciding on revisions.

Task Team Lead: Agustín Samano
Region: Malaysia (pilot) → Southeast Asia (extension)
Timeline: US Benchmark January 2026; Malaysia Pilot February 2026; Multi-Country Dataset June 2026

1 Motivation
Fiscal policy is one of governments’ most powerful levers for macroeconomic stability and private-sector development. Yet for most emerging markets we lack the cornerstone input required for credible analysis: consistent and comparable measures of exogenous fiscal shocks1.
1 See for example Mertens and Ravn (2013) and Romer and Romer (2010)The United States is the only country where this has been done systematically, through the “narrative approach” pioneered by Romer and Romer (2010). Their method uses historical documents—budget speeches, economic reports, legislative records—to identify why taxes or spending changed, and to distinguish exogenous shocks from policy actions responding to the business cycle.
Replicating this approach manually is costly. For low- and middle-income countries, it has simply never been feasible.
Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) make this possible for the first time. These models can read long historical documents, interpret motivations, and extract structured information at scale—opening the door to producing robust fiscal shock series for developing countries.
This project proposes to build a validated LLM-assisted methodology for fiscal shock identification, starting with Malaysia and extending across Southeast Asia.

2 Novelty and Innovation

A. A US-Validated Codebook System
The project develops 4 domain-specific codebooks (C1-C4) validated on 44 US fiscal acts with Romer & Romer’s original labels using the Halterman & Keith (2025) validation framework. This ensures:

we anchor the method in a gold-standard dataset,
we quantify codebook accuracy against known results via rigorous behavioral testing,
we design country-agnostic definitions that transfer across countries.

This step is essential for credibility: codebooks must demonstrate strong performance on US data before cross-country deployment.


B. Transfer Learning with Expert Validation
We deploy the US-validated codebooks to Malaysia, where English-language archives are extensive. The pipeline:

identifies fiscal measures meeting the “significant mention” rule (Codebook C1),
classifies motivations using Romer & Romer’s categories (Codebook C2),
extracts implementation timing using the midpoint rule (Codebook C3),
extracts fiscal impact magnitude (Codebook C4), and
integrates systematic expert validation following Halterman & Keith error analysis methodology.

Once methodology is validated on Malaysia (estimated 20-40 acts, 1980-2022), the pipeline extends to Indonesia, Thailand, the Philippines, and Vietnam, with built-in multilingual capability.

3 Project Outputs (June 2026)

A. New Data Assets

Narrative fiscal episode datasets for 5 Southeast Asian economies (estimated 20-60 acts per country depending on political stability and archive quality).
Expert-validated shock-event datasets identifying exogenous tax and spending changes: timing, size, and motivation.
Harmonized multi-country panel suitable for macro and micro analysis.



B. Analytical Products

Macro impulse responses estimated with local projections.
Firm-level effects (investment, employment) using modern LP-DiD methods (Dube et al., n.d.).
A methodological paper on LLM-assisted narrative identification, similar to (Romer and Romer, n.d.).



C. Tooling and Capacity

A fully documented, open-source pipeline (R-based).
Country-agnostic LLM codebooks, rules, and evaluation protocols.
A tested architecture that can be extended to Africa, South Asia, and Latin America.

4 Implementation Plan

Phase 0 – Codebook Development (Jan 2026)
Develop and validate 4 codebooks (C1-C4) on 44 US fiscal acts using the Halterman & Keith framework: codebook preparation, behavioral testing, zero-shot evaluation, and error analysis. Validate performance against Romer & Romer ground truth.


Phase 1 – US Full Production (Jan-Feb 2026)
Run validated codebooks on the full US document corpus. Compare end-to-end results against Romer & Romer’s published shock series to confirm production readiness.


Phase 2 – Malaysia Pilot (Feb-Mar 2026)
Deploy US-validated codebooks to Malaysia documents (1980-2022), generating candidate fiscal shock dataset. Engage local expert to validate outputs and assess cross-country transfer performance. Refine codebook definitions based on expert feedback.


Phase 3 – Southeast Asia Scaling (June 2026)
Extend to Indonesia, Thailand, the Philippines, and Vietnam, incorporating expert review and news-based cross-checks.

5 Risk Management







Risk
Mitigation




Limited training data (44 US acts)
Few-shot learning; expert validation loop; focus on methodology over scale.


Weak or uneven archives
Combine parliamentary, budget, and press sources; clearly document gaps.


Cross-country transfer failure
Expert validation quantifies performance; error analysis informs refinement.


LLM misclassification
US benchmarking + systematic expert review; conservative thresholds for exogeneity.


Translation quality for non-English sources
Native multilingual LLMs (Claude/GPT-4); manual review of samples.


Political bias in source documents
Independent cross-validation with contemporaneous news archives.

section: 6 Strategic Value for the World Bank
text:
6 Strategic Value for the World Bank

Transforms a previously infeasible task into a reproducible method for potentially dozens of client countries.
Provides a missing input for macroeconomic modeling enabling better fiscal forecasting and policy advice.
Offers the first systematic estimates of firm-level impacts of fiscal policy in Southeast Asia using modern causal methods.
Establishes the World Bank as a pioneer in responsible, auditable LLM use for economic analysis.
Creates a transferable framework that can serve as a global public good for future fiscal reform programs.

Conclusion
This project brings together frontier methodology, practical policy relevance, and realistic scalability. The US-benchmarked codebook system (C1-C4) demonstrates the Halterman & Keith validation framework applied to economic history; the Malaysia pilot tests cross-country transfer with expert validation; and the multi-country rollout provides methodology replicable beyond Southeast Asia. The contribution is methodological: showing country-agnostic codebooks can assist experts in fiscal shock identification, reducing effort from months to weeks, even with limited training data.

title: Scaling Narrative Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
Task Team Lead: Agustín Samano
Country/Region: United States (training), Malaysia (pilot), Southeast Asia (extension)
Timeline:

title: Scaling Narrative Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
1 Introduction and Motivation
Fiscal policy plays a central role in macroeconomic stabilization, long-run growth, and private-sector development. Yet we lack credible and comparable exogenous fiscal shock series for most emerging markets. Existing international evidence is heavily skewed toward the United States and a few OECD countries, due to the high cost of constructing narrative datasets like those of Romer and Romer (2010) or Mertens and Ravn (2013).
Romer and Romer’s narrative analysis of postwar US tax changes provides act-by-act classifications of tax legislation, their motivations (spending-driven, countercyclical, deficit-driven, long-run), and the timing and size of revenue effects. These labels are exactly the kind of supervised signal that modern language models can learn from.
The rise of Large Language Models (LLMs) offers a practical pathway to extend the narrative approach systematically to developing countries. Governments now publish large volumes of digitized legislative documents, budget speeches, and parliamentary transcripts, often in multiple languages. Manually processing these sources is infeasible; LLMs can drastically reduce the bottleneck if they are trained and validated against high-quality human benchmarks first.
This project proposes to:

Develop and validate a codebook-based LLM system on the US using the Romer & Romer tax shock dataset and the Halterman & Keith (2025) validation framework.
Adapt and validate this system to Malaysia as a pilot country.
Scale to Indonesia, Thailand, the Philippines, and Vietnam, combining LLMs, multilingual translation, and human validation.

The outputs will be:

A US-based benchmark module: Four validated codebooks (C1-C4) that can reproduce US narrative tax shocks from raw documents.
A narrative episode dataset for each SEA country based on executive, legislative, and press documents.
A shock-event dataset representing actual changes in fiscal liabilities or expenditure paths, classified into the Romer and Romer (2010) motivation categories.
A set of macro and firm-level impulse responses to fiscal shocks using local projections and modern difference-in-differences methods (LP-DiD).

These will constitute the first systematic narrative fiscal shock series for Southeast Asia, backed by an explicit US benchmark.

title: Scaling Narrative Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
2 Research Questions
The project focuses on four core questions:

US Benchmarking / Codebook Validation: Can LLM codebooks validated on the US narrative corpus and Romer & Romer’s tax-shock labels reliably:

identify fiscal measures meeting the “significant mention” rule,
classify their motivations, and
recover the timing and size of tax shocks closely enough to reproduce known US fiscal multipliers?

Measurement in Emerging Markets: Conditional on a successful US benchmark, can we construct reliable narrative-based fiscal shock series for Southeast Asian economies using the same country-agnostic codebooks, multilingual translation, and human validation?
Validation and Transportability:

How closely do codebook-based classifications align with (i) expert assessments and (ii) contemporaneous news coverage in each country?
Are the identified exogenous shocks orthogonal to short-run macro conditions?
How much degradation (if any) occurs when moving from US to Malaysia and from Malaysia to other SEA contexts?

Impact: What are the dynamic effects of tax and spending shocks on:

GDP, investment, FDI, employment, revenue, and expenditure?
Firm-level outcomes such as investment and employment?

title: Scaling Narrative Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
3 Proposed Approach
We structure the work in three phases, with a deliberate Phase 0 on the US to de-risk the entire pipeline.

Phase 0: US Codebook Development and Benchmarking (January 2026)
Objective: Use the US as a sandbox to develop and validate LLM codebooks using the Halterman & Keith (2025) framework before touching emerging market data.

Data

Romer & Romer’s narrative dataset of postwar US federal tax actions (timing, size, motivation, present-value paths).
Underlying narrative sources:

Economic Report of the President
Budget of the United States Government
Treasury Annual Reports
Presidential speeches and statements
Congressional reports and debates




Tasks

Corpus reconstruction and alignment

Reconstruct the narrative corpus used in Romer and Romer (2010).
Align each tax act in the Romer & Romer dataset with the source passages used to classify its motivation and revenue effects.

Codebook C1: Measure Identification

Develop a machine-readable codebook to identify fiscal measures meeting the R&R “significant mention” rule.
Validate using H&K behavioral tests: legal outputs (100%), memorization (100%), order sensitivity (<5%).
Target: Recall ≥90%, Precision ≥80% on 44 labeled US acts.

Codebook C2: Motivation Classification
Using Romer & Romer’s four-way motivation: spending-driven, countercyclical, deficit-driven, long-run/structural,

Develop a codebook with explicit criteria and positive/negative examples following H&K format.
Embed best-practice rules for narrative work (real-time sources, explicit criteria, documentation) from Romer & Romer’s 2023 Presidential Address.
Target: Weighted F1 ≥70%, Exogenous Precision ≥85%.

Codebooks C3 and C4: Timing and Magnitude Extraction

C3 (Timing): Extract implementation quarter(s) using R&R midpoint rule. Target: Exact quarter ≥85%, ±1 quarter ≥95%.
C4 (Magnitude): Extract fiscal impact in billions USD. Target: MAPE <30%, Sign accuracy ≥95%.
Compare extracted values with Romer & Romer’s act-quarter series.

H&K Validation Pipeline
Each codebook passes through the Halterman & Keith 5-stage framework:

S0: Codebook preparation (YAML definitions, examples)
S1: Behavioral tests (sanity checks)
S2: Zero-shot evaluation (LOOCV on 44 US acts)
S3: Error analysis (failure mode identification)
S4: Fine-tuning (last resort, avoided to preserve cross-country transferability)

US Back-Testing

Re-estimate US tax multipliers using only codebook-generated episodes and shocks:

aggregate, exogenous tax changes à la Romer and Romer (2010),
dynamic effects via local projections.

Benchmark whether:

sign and timing match the original estimates,
magnitudes are within acceptable bands (e.g., ±20–30%).

This gives a quantitative yardstick for “good enough” codebook performance.


Deliverable of Phase 0: Four validated codebooks (C1-C4) that can (i) reconstruct the US series reasonably well and (ii) be deployed to other countries without retraining.



Phase 1: Malaysia Pilot (February 2026)
Malaysia is the optimal starting point due to extensive English-language archives and relatively stable political institutions (1980-2022, estimated 20-40 fiscal acts).

Data Sources

Parliamentary transcripts, Hansards
Budget speeches and fiscal policy statements
Bills and explanatory memoranda
Prime Minister / Minister of Finance speeches
Official press releases
Secondary validation archives (international/local newspapers)




Narrative Identification Strategy
The project distinguishes two levels of analysis:

Narrative Episodes (Talk) All instances where policymakers discuss tax or spending actions. Output: a comprehensive episode-level dataset with motivation labels.
Shock Events (Action) Subset of episodes that result in measurable changes to fiscal liabilities/expenditures (legislated or executive). Output: a shock-event dataset for econometric analysis (timing, size, tax base, motivation).



Codebook Pipeline (Human-in-the-Loop)
We deploy the US-validated codebooks and adapt them to Malaysia as needed.
Step 1 — Ingestion & Parsing Automatic collection and segmentation of documents into candidate narrative episodes, using Codebook C1 (Measure Identification) adjusted to Malaysian document formats.
Step 2 — Translation LLM-based translation for non-English texts into English, with:

consistent terminology for fiscal concepts,
retention of uncertainty / qualifications in the original text.

Step 3 — Motivation Classification (Codebook C2)
Each identified measure is classified into Romer & Romer’s four motivation categories using the US-validated C2 codebook:

Spending-driven
Countercyclical
Deficit-driven
Long-run/structural

The codebook produces:

a proposed label,
a short justification using explicit criteria (e.g., “responding to current macro weakness” vs “paying for a new program”),
a confidence score,
an “inconsistency flag” comparing stated motives (in-text) to inferred motives (based on macro context).

Step 4 — Expert Validation & Codebook Adaptation
Following the H&K S3 error analysis methodology:

Draw stratified samples by label, confidence, time period, and document type.
Local experts review and re-label:

obvious misclassifications (e.g., misreading of local institutional language),
ambiguous cases (e.g., mixed motivations).

Use corrections to:

update codebook definitions (S0 revision),
add Malaysia-specific examples to clarifications.


Step 5 — Timing and Magnitude Extraction (Codebooks C3 & C4)

Apply C3 (Timing) and C4 (Magnitude) codebooks to extract:

change in liabilities (quarter, size, tax base),
present value of announced changes,
exogenous vs endogenous classification (based on motivation and timing, as in Romer and Romer (2010)).




Phase 2: Extension to Indonesia, Thailand, the Philippines, Vietnam (June 2026)

Apply the same codebook pipeline, with:

country-specific corpus discovery and scraping,
language adaptation (translation and terminology),
targeted human validation in each country.

Leverage country-agnostic codebook design:

US → Malaysia serves as the main validation of cross-country transfer,
Malaysia → other SEA countries leverages experience with mixed English/local language environments and similar fiscal institutions.
Codebook definitions (S0) may be revised based on expert feedback, but core methodology remains unchanged.

title: Scaling Narrative Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
4 Validation
The project embeds a rigorous, multi-method validation strategy, now explicitly in two layers: US and SEA.

1. US Benchmark Validation (H&K Framework)

Apply Halterman & Keith 5-stage validation to each codebook:

S1: Behavioral tests (legal outputs, memorization, order sensitivity)
S2: Zero-shot evaluation via LOOCV on 44 US acts
S3: Error analysis identifying systematic failure patterns

Compare codebook-derived US shocks to Romer & Romer’s original series:

confusion matrix for motivation labels,
distribution of timing errors (quarter misalignment),
revenue size errors (absolute and relative).

Re-run baseline US macro regressions using:

local projections for GDP, investment, and unemployment,
exogenous tax changes only.

Require that impulse response functions are qualitatively identical and quantitatively close to original published estimates before deploying codebooks to SEA.



2. Expert Review in SEA (H&K S3 Error Analysis)

Engage World Bank economists (including KL office) and local experts.
Evaluate a stratified sample of episodes and shocks in each country.
Document disagreements systematically using H&K error taxonomy to refine codebook definitions.
Target: Expert agreement ≥80% on measure identification, ≥70% on motivation classification.



3. News-Based Cross-Validation

Use institutional access (e.g. ITAM, WB) to global and regional newspaper archives.
Assess whether contemporaneous reporting aligns with classified motivations, especially for:

sharp tax hikes or cuts,
politically sensitive episodes,
“borderline” exogenous vs endogenous cases.




4. Internal Consistency Checks

Test correlation of “exogenous” shocks with real-time macro indicators (GDP, unemployment, inflation).
Exogenous shocks should show weak contemporaneous correlation with short-run macro conditions; if not, re-examine classification.

This two-tier validation (US + SEA) turns the US into a truth-benchmark and SEA into the test of codebook portability.

title: Scaling Narrative Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
5 Empirical Analysis

1. Macro-Level Effects
Use local projections to estimate dynamic responses of:

GDP growth
Private investment
FDI
Employment
Government revenue and expenditure

Separate estimates will be produced for:

tax vs spending shocks,
personal vs corporate income tax shocks (where identifiable),
exogenous vs endogenous shocks.



2. Firm-Level Effects
Using firm-level panels (e.g. Orbis, national administrative data where available), estimate how fiscal shocks affect:

investment,
employment,
capital formation.

Identification:

LP-DiD (Local Projections DiD) following Dube et al. (2022), which unifies local projections with clean-control difference-in-differences in staggered treatment settings.
Treatment assignment based on exposure (e.g., sector-level tax incidence, import/export orientation, foreign ownership).

This will generate the first micro-based fiscal multipliers for these countries.

title: Scaling Narrative Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
6 Outputs and Deliverables

By January 2026 (US Benchmark)

Reconstructed US narrative corpus aligned with Romer & Romer tax acts.
Four validated codebooks (C1-C4) with H&K documentation:

C1: Measure identification,
C2: Motivation classification,
C3: Timing extraction,
C4: Magnitude extraction.

Quantitative comparison of codebook-based vs original US tax multipliers.



By February 2026 (Malaysia Pilot Completion)

Fully operational R-based and codebook-assisted pipeline for Malaysia.
Narrative episode dataset (Malaysia).
First version of the shock-event dataset (Malaysia).
Expert validation report with agreement metrics and error analysis.



By June 2026 (Core Dataset Completion)

Narrative and shock-event datasets for: Malaysia, Indonesia, Thailand, the Philippines, Vietnam.
Harmonized multi-country episode and shock datasets.
Fully validated codebook framework (C1-C4) with country-specific adaptations documented.
Public dissemination-ready code and documentation (subject to licensing/permissions).



Post-2026 (Analytical Outputs)

Macro-level impact study of tax and spending shocks in SEA.
Firm-level impact study using LP-DiD.
Methodological paper on codebook-assisted narrative identification, emphasizing:

H&K validation framework applied to economic history,
cross-country transferability of country-agnostic codebooks,
human-in-the-loop design with expert validation.

Data package for public release (subject to permissions).

title: Scaling Narrative Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
7 Risks and Mitigation







Risk
Mitigation




Incomplete/uneven archives
Combine multiple sources; transparently document coverage; adjust sample windows.


Translation inaccuracies
Dedicated translation stage; manual checks; standardized prompts and glossaries.


Codebook misclassification
H&K validation framework; human-in-the-loop refinement; conservative thresholds for exogeneity.


Domain shift US → SEA
Country-agnostic codebook design; codebook definition revision (S0) based on expert feedback; avoid fine-tuning to preserve transferability.


Political bias in narrative documents
Cross-check with news; inconsistency flags; robust alternative classifications.


Weak identification for some countries
Use Malaysia as a strong pilot; downweight uncertain periods; exploit LP-DiD with clean controls.

title: Scaling Narrative Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
section: 8 Policy Relevance and Value Added to the World Bank
text:
8 Policy Relevance and Value Added to the World Bank

Fills critical data gaps for fiscal-policy modeling in major middle-income economies.
Provides scalable infrastructure for narrative shock identification, with US-validated codebooks exportable to other regions (Latin America, South Asia, Africa).
Enhances understanding of how fiscal policy affects private investment and firm behavior, central to the Bank’s structural reform and growth agenda.
Supports better macroeconomic forecasting and policy advice in client countries by plugging a key missing input: credible exogenous fiscal shocks.

The project also showcases responsible, auditable use of LLMs for applied economic research—grounded in best-practice narrative methods (R&R) and rigorous validation frameworks (H&K)—rather than opaque black-box automation.

title: Working with Claude Code in This Project
text:
This project uses Claude Code extensively: for writing R functions, managing a reproducible pipeline, developing LLM codebooks, and maintaining documentation. Claude Code can do most of the tasks involved in empirical research. The harder question is what it should do versus what the human researcher must own.
This document answers that question for this project. It starts with principles, then maps those principles onto concrete systems we’ve built.

title: Working with Claude Code in This Project
text:
This project uses Claude Code extensively: for writing R functions, managing a reproducible pipeline, developing LLM codebooks, and maintaining documentation. Claude Code can do most of the tasks involved in empirical research. The harder question is what it should do versus what the human researcher must own.
This document answers that question for this project. It starts with principles, then maps those principles onto concrete systems we’ve built.

title: Working with Claude Code in This Project
text:
Part 1: Principles

The chain of human ownership
Four responsibilities belong to the human researcher. They form a chain: each one depends on the ones before it.
1. Meaning. The human both receives and confers meaning. Receiving: understanding why fiscal policy’s impact on growth, employment, and investment in emerging economies matters. Conferring: deciding that these numbers in the dataset are exogenous fiscal shocks that causally identify fiscal policy. An LLM can pattern-match against codebook definitions, but it does not know what exogeneity means for causal identification. It does not understand that getting this wrong invalidates the entire downstream analysis. Knowledge does not exist until someone claims it and can be held accountable for the claim.
2. Learning. The human must learn enough to own the work. This is not a moral imperative but a practical one. If you defer all intellectual engagement to the end, you face a misaligned incentive to share output you do not understand. The project infrastructure should be a commitment device that forces intellectual engagement at the right moments. Not everywhere, but at every point that is central to the contribution.
A heuristic for deciding where to engage deeply: how central is the method to the research contribution? PDF extraction is instrumental; you can delegate it fully and verify outputs against your mental model of what well-extracted text looks like. But codebook design is the intellectual contribution of this project. You must understand in detail how and why the codebooks work, and when they might not. Delegate the instrumental. Own the core.
3. Credibility. The depth of your involvement determines the depth at which you can credibly defend your work. When a referee asks “why did you use a detection framing instead of topic classification for C1?”, you need to have lived through iteration 9’s catastrophic 29.1% recall, understood the dominant-topic heuristic diagnosis, and made the decision to reframe. You choose where to go deep and where to delegate, but you own the consequences of that choice.
4. Decision ownership. Claude can analyze, propose, challenge, and trace implications. But the commit (“we will do this, not that”) belongs to the human. Decisions are auditable in ways that judgment is not. The iteration log records every decision with a date, context, and human rationale. Someone can examine whether the decision was well-reasoned. The person who made the decision faces the consequences: if removing the enacted filter causes problems downstream, that is your problem to solve. Claude proposed it, you approved it, your name is on the paper.


The core heuristic
AI owns tasks where errors are recoverable. Humans own tasks where errors compound silently.
Code has tests. Metrics can be recomputed. YAML can be re-validated. If Claude gets these wrong, you find out quickly and fix it cheaply.
A bad research design does not throw an error. A wrong interpretation gets baked into the next iteration’s decision field and shapes all future work. A cost incurrence cannot be un-spent. The infrastructure we have built forces human involvement precisely at these high-consequence points.


What honest research with AI looks like
The heuristics we have built are mechanisms for honesty:

The iteration log records failures, not just successes. Eleven iterations, eight of which failed.
The delta log forces you to acknowledge when implementation contradicts your plan, rather than quietly drifting.
Strategy sync makes you justify decisions under adversarial questioning, not just rationalize them after the fact.
One change at a time prevents hiding behind a bundle of changes where you cannot tell what worked.
The pre-flight checklist prevents running experiments on uncommitted code where you cannot reproduce the result.

These are not efficiency tools. They are integrity infrastructure. They make it harder to do sloppy research, even accidentally.

title: Working with Claude Code in This Project
text:
Part 2: Architecture

The strategy document as constitution
docs/strategy.md is the single source of truth for research methodology. Every agent, skill, and review process references it. It specifies the four codebooks (C1–C4), the five-stage validation pipeline (S0–S3), success criteria, and implementation sequencing.
The strategy document lives in a three-tier documentation system that enforces the separation between human specification and machine context:

Tier 1 (auto-update): CLAUDE.md files. These are execution context for Claude Code. Claude updates them directly when implementation changes make them stale. They track current status, file inventories, and target definitions.
Tier 2 (never auto-edit): docs/strategy.md, docs/proposal.qmd, docs/two_pager.qmd. These represent deliberate research design decisions by the human. Claude never edits them directly. Instead, bottom-up discoveries are logged as entries in docs/deltas.md, where they accumulate until the human reconciles them via /strategy-sync.
Tier 3 (ignore): Stable reference documents, generated artifacts, and infrastructure docs that rarely need updates.

Why this matters: The human owns the research design. The AI owns the execution context. Changes flow upward through the delta log, never through autonomous edits to the strategy.


The agent system
The project uses 10 specialized Claude Code agents in .claude/agents/, organized by function and deliberately constrained:
Read-only agents (cannot modify files):








Agent
Model
Role




code-reviewer
Haiku
R code quality check


fiscal-policy-specialist
Sonnet
Romer & Romer domain expert


llm-eval-specialist
Sonnet
Halterman & Keith evaluation expert


strategy-reviewer
Sonnet
Verify implementation matches strategy.md


notebook-reviewer
Sonnet
Verify notebooks evaluate what they should



Write-capable agents (can create and modify files):








Agent
Model
Role




codebook-developer
Sonnet
Draft YAML codebooks, iterate on definitions


r-coder
Sonnet
Write R functions for the pipeline


pipeline-manager
Sonnet
Manage targets pipeline definitions


doc-writer
Sonnet
Write Quarto documents


document-extractor
Haiku
PDF text extraction



Key design patterns:

Capability restriction matches role. A reviewer that can also edit files is a developer pretending to review. Read-only agents are explicitly limited to Read, Grep, Glob.
Model tiering. Haiku for cheap, narrow tasks (code review, PDF extraction). Sonnet for complex tasks requiring deeper reasoning.
Consultation pattern. The strategy-reviewer consults fiscal-policy-specialist and llm-eval-specialist rather than trying to be an expert in everything. Specialists advise; the reviewer synthesizes.



The skill system
Skills are procedures codified as prompts, invoked via slash commands. They are not about giving Claude new capabilities. They enforce a repeatable workflow and prevent the AI from skipping steps.








Skill
Trigger
What it enforces




/pre-flight
Before tar_make()
7-check validation: git clean, target exists, dependencies current, codebook valid, API key set, cost estimate


/review-iteration
After pipeline run
Structured read-only diagnosis. Ends with “Do NOT propose code changes.”


/log-iteration
After discussing results
Auto-gathers metadata (version, git hash, date), asks human for interpretation and decision, appends YAML entry


/doc-sync
After significant changes
Updates Tier 1 docs, logs deltas for Tier 2 docs. Nudges at 3+ unresolved deltas


/strategy-sync
At stage gates or 3+ deltas
Adversarial reconciliation: Claude challenges, human justifies, rationale recorded


/progress-report
For external communication
Generates dated .qmd with auto-gathered state and terminology translation





The pipeline
The project uses {targets} for reproducible data processing. Two rules are absolute:

All data generation goes through the pipeline. No standalone scripts that create data files. No saveRDS() or write_csv() outside targets. This ensures reproducibility, caching, and lineage.
No autonomous API calls. Claude never runs tar_make() on API-calling targets without explicit human approval. Read-only operations (tar_read(), tar_outdated(), tar_visnetwork()) are always safe.

The pipeline tracks codebook YAML files as format = "file" targets, so editing a codebook automatically invalidates all downstream results.

title: Working with Claude Code in This Project
text:
Part 3: The Iteration Cycle

The daily workflow
The core loop for codebook development:
1. Edit codebook YAML (one component at a time)
2. /pre-flight          → verify everything is ready
3. tar_make(<target>)   → human authorizes the run
4. /review-iteration    → structured diagnosis (read-only)
5. Discuss results      → human interprets, decides next step
6. /log-iteration       → record what happened and why
7. Repeat or advance to next stage
Each step has a clear owner. Steps 1, 3, 5, and 7 are human decisions. Steps 2, 4, and 6 are Claude-assisted procedures that enforce rigor without taking control.


The documentation lifecycle
Implementation discoveries feed back into the research design through a structured cycle:
strategy.md (human intent)
    ↓ implementation work
docs/deltas.md (bottom-up discoveries via /doc-sync)
    ↓ accumulation trigger (3+ entries or stage gate)
/strategy-sync (adversarial reconciliation)
    ↓ disposition: incorporate / acknowledge / defer
strategy.md (updated) + audit trail (rationale recorded)
The strategy document stays alive rather than becoming a fossil from week one.


Exploration vs. production runs
Not all pipeline runs are equal. This project distinguishes:

Exploration runs use cheap models (Qwen, Llama via OpenRouter) to test infrastructure, debug pipeline mechanics, and estimate costs. Results are not reportable.
Production runs use the validated model (Claude Haiku) for formal evaluation. Results feed the iteration log and inform codebook decisions.

The iteration log explicitly marks each run’s model and provider. A comment in _targets.R states: “Non-Anthropic providers are for cost/feasibility exploration only. Mixing providers invalidates stage comparability.”


The 10 workflow conventions
These rules prevent recurring friction patterns identified during codebook development. Each exists because we learned the hard way.

Plan-first mode. When asked to diagnose or investigate, present findings and wait. Do not implement changes unless explicitly told to. Why: prevents Claude from “fixing” things before the human understands the problem.
Root cause first. Identify the root cause before proposing a fix. Do not patch symptoms. Why: iteration 3 taught us that a test failure was caused by bad codebook examples, not a test implementation bug. Fixing the test would have hidden the real problem.
Model ID validation. Verify model IDs against known valid values before writing them. Why: stale model IDs cause silent pipeline failures that waste debugging time.
Prefer existing files. Search with Glob/Grep before creating new files. Why: duplicate implementations create maintenance burden and inconsistency.
No autonomous API calls. Never run tar_make() on API-calling targets without explicit human approval. Why: API calls cost money and change pipeline state. The human must authorize both.
Commit before pipeline runs. Ensure no uncommitted changes to codebook YAML or R functions before running. Why: the iteration log stores git hashes. Uncommitted changes break reproducibility.
One change at a time. Change one codebook component per iteration. Why: makes the iteration log interpretable and supports ablation-style reasoning. If you change three things and recall improves, you do not know which change helped.
Pipeline data validation. After tar_make() completes, verify result shape with tar_read(<target>) |> str(). Why: catches silent data issues before they propagate downstream.
Quarto render safety. Render specific files, never the full project. Why: prevents accidentally re-rendering expensive notebooks or breaking unrelated documents.
Strategy reconciliation. After stage gate crossings or when 3+ unresolved deltas accumulate, run /strategy-sync. Why: implementation drift is inevitable. This forces periodic reconciliation where Claude challenges your reasoning and you justify decisions on the record.



Multi-agent workflow patterns
Three reusable patterns for parallel agent coordination:

Pattern 1: Codebook review. Two specialist agents (fiscal-policy-specialist + llm-eval-specialist) review a proposed codebook change in parallel. The main session synthesizes their feedback for the user.
Pattern 2: Pre-pipeline validation. pipeline-manager + strategy-reviewer verify target readiness in parallel before the user runs tar_make().
Pattern 3: Post-stage documentation. doc-writer + notebook-reviewer update and verify a notebook in parallel after a stage gate is crossed.

title: Working with Claude Code in This Project
text:
Part 4: Reference

Key file locations



Path
Purpose




docs/strategy.md
Authoritative methodology (Tier 2)


docs/deltas.md
Bottom-up implementation discoveries


CLAUDE.md
Project instructions for Claude Code (Tier 1)


_targets.R
Pipeline definition


prompts/c1_measure_id.yml
C1 codebook (active)


prompts/iterations/c1.yml
C1 iteration log


.claude/agents/
Agent configurations


.claude/skills/
Skill definitions


R/
Pipeline functions


notebooks/
Analysis and verification notebooks


reports/
Dated progress reports





Common commands
# Pipeline operations (safe, read-only)
tar_read(target_name)        # Read a target's output
tar_outdated()               # Check what needs updating
tar_visnetwork()             # Visualize dependency graph

# Pipeline execution (requires human authorization)
tar_make(target_name)        # Run a specific target

# Quarto (render specific files only)
quarto render notebooks/c1_measure_id.qmd


Skill quick reference







Command
When to use




/pre-flight
Before any tar_make() on API-calling targets


/review-iteration
After a pipeline stage completes, before deciding what to change


/log-iteration
After discussing results, to record the iteration


/doc-sync
After significant implementation work


/strategy-sync
At stage gates or when 3+ deltas accumulate


/progress-report
When preparing updates for external stakeholders





The iteration log schema
Each entry in prompts/iterations/<codebook>.yml records:
- iteration: 11
  codebook_version: "0.3.0"
  date: "2026-03-03"
  git_commit: "62495fb"
  model: "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"
  provider: "anthropic"
  stage: "s2"
  changes: >
    What changed in the codebook since last iteration.
  results:
    overall_pass: false
    metrics:
      - metric: "combined_recall"
        value: 0.8113
        target: 0.9000
        pass: false
  interpretation: >
    What these results mean. Written by the human.
  decision: >
    What to do next. Decided by the human.
Every past codebook version is recoverable: git show <hash>:prompts/c1_measure_id.yml.

title: Working with Claude Code in This Project
text:
This project uses Claude Code extensively: for writing pipeline functions, managing a reproducible data workflow, developing measurement instruments, and maintaining documentation. Claude Code can perform most of the technical tasks involved in empirical research. The harder question is what it should do versus what the human researcher must own.
This document answers that question. It starts with principles, then maps those principles onto the concrete systems built into this template.

title: Working with Claude Code in This Project
text:
This project uses Claude Code extensively: for writing pipeline functions, managing a reproducible data workflow, developing measurement instruments, and maintaining documentation. Claude Code can perform most of the technical tasks involved in empirical research. The harder question is what it should do versus what the human researcher must own.
This document answers that question. It starts with principles, then maps those principles onto the concrete systems built into this template.

title: Working with Claude Code in This Project
text:
Part 1: Principles

The chain of human ownership
Four responsibilities belong to the human researcher. They form a chain: each depends on the ones before it.
1. Meaning. The human both receives and confers meaning. Receiving: understanding why the research question matters and what is at stake if the answer is wrong. Conferring: deciding that the model’s output — these labels, these estimates, these classifications — are what the instrument says they are, and accepting accountability for that claim. An LLM can pattern-match against instrument definitions, but it does not know what the classification means for causal identification or external validity. It does not understand that getting this wrong could invalidate the downstream analysis. Knowledge does not exist until someone claims it and can be held accountable.
2. Learning. The human must learn enough to own the work. This is not a moral imperative but a practical one. If you defer all intellectual engagement to the end, you face a misaligned incentive to share output you do not understand. The project infrastructure should be a commitment device that forces intellectual engagement at the right moments — not everywhere, but at every point central to the research contribution.
A useful heuristic: how central is this task to the contribution? Data pipeline plumbing is instrumental; delegate it fully and verify outputs against your mental model. But instrument design is the intellectual contribution. You must understand in detail how and why the instruments work, and when they might not. Delegate the instrumental. Own the core.
3. Credibility. The depth of your involvement determines the depth at which you can credibly defend your work. You need to have lived through the iteration history — the failures, the diagnostic decisions, the tradeoffs chosen and why — to defend them under peer review. The iteration log makes that history legible. You choose where to go deep, but you own the consequences.
4. Decision ownership. Claude can analyze, propose, challenge, and trace implications. But the commit (“we will do this, not that”) belongs to the human. Decisions are auditable in ways that judgment is not. The iteration log records every decision with a date, context, and rationale. The person who made the decision faces the consequences if it was wrong.


The core heuristic
AI owns tasks where errors are recoverable. Humans own tasks where errors compound silently.
Code has tests. Metrics can be recomputed. YAML can be re-validated. If Claude gets these wrong, you find out quickly and fix it cheaply.
A bad research design does not throw an error. A wrong interpretation gets baked into the next iteration’s decision field and shapes all future work. A cost incurrence cannot be un-spent. The infrastructure in this template forces human involvement precisely at these high-consequence points.


What honest research with AI looks like
The mechanisms built into this template are tools for honesty:

The iteration log records failures, not just successes. It is how reviewers know you actually tested the instrument rather than picking the first version that worked.
The delta log forces you to acknowledge when implementation contradicts your plan, rather than quietly drifting.
Strategy sync makes you justify decisions under adversarial questioning, not rationalize them after the fact.
One change at a time prevents hiding behind a bundle of changes where you cannot tell what worked.
The pre-flight checklist prevents running experiments on uncommitted code where you cannot reproduce the result.

These are not efficiency tools. They are integrity infrastructure.

title: Working with Claude Code in This Project
text:
Part 2: Architecture

The strategy document as constitution
docs/strategy.md is the single source of truth for research methodology. Every agent, skill, and review process references it. It specifies your measurement instruments, validation pipeline, success criteria, and implementation sequencing.
The strategy document lives in a three-tier documentation system that enforces the separation between human specification and machine context:

Tier 1 (auto-update): CLAUDE.md files. These are execution context for Claude Code. Claude updates them directly when implementation changes make them stale. They track current status, file inventories, and pipeline definitions.
Tier 2 (never auto-edit): docs/strategy.md and any other documents designated Tier 2 in your CLAUDE.md. These represent deliberate research design decisions. Claude never edits them directly. Instead, bottom-up discoveries are logged as entries in docs/deltas.md, where they accumulate until you reconcile them via /strategy-sync.
Tier 3 (ignore): Stable reference documents, generated artifacts, and infrastructure docs that rarely need updates.

Why this matters: You own the research design. The AI owns the execution context. Changes flow upward through the delta log, never through autonomous edits to the strategy.


The agent system
The project uses specialized Claude Code agents in .claude/agents/, organized by function and deliberately constrained:

Read-only agents (limited to Read, Grep, Glob): reviewers, domain specialists, verifiers. A reviewer that can also edit files is a developer pretending to review.
Write-capable agents: developers, builders, documenters.
Model tiering: cheap fast models for narrow tasks; more capable models for tasks requiring reasoning across domains.

See COMPANION.md for the full agent taxonomy and design principles. See CLAUDE.md for your project-specific agent configurations.


The skill system
Skills are procedures codified as prompts, invoked via slash commands. They enforce a repeatable workflow and prevent steps from being skipped.








Skill
When to use
What it enforces




/setup-project
Once, from template
Onboarding interview → draft CLAUDE.md


/doc-sync
After significant work
Tier 1 updates + delta logging + nudge at 3+ deltas


/strategy-sync
Stage gates or 3+ deltas
Adversarial reconciliation


/log-iteration
After pipeline stage
Auto-gathered metadata + human interpretation/decision


/progress-report
External communication
Auto-gathered state + terminology translation


/quarto-style
Writing .qmd files
Formatting authority





The pipeline
Two pipeline rules are absolute:

All data generation goes through the pipeline. No standalone scripts, no manual saveRDS() or write_csv() outside the pipeline tool. Reproducibility requires tracked dependencies.
No autonomous API calls. Claude never runs the pipeline on API-calling targets without explicit human approval. Read-only operations are always safe.

title: Working with Claude Code in This Project
text:
Part 3: The Iteration Cycle

The daily workflow
The core loop for instrument development:
1. Edit instrument definition (one component at a time)
2. /pre-flight          → verify everything is ready
3. tar_make(<target>)   → human authorizes the run
4. /review-iteration    → structured diagnosis (read-only)
5. Discuss results      → human interprets, decides next step
6. /log-iteration       → record what happened and why
7. Repeat or advance to next stage
Steps 1, 3, 5, and 7 are human decisions. Steps 2, 4, and 6 are Claude-assisted procedures that enforce rigor without taking control.


The iteration log as lab notebook
If you have worked in Stata or R, you know the problem: you changed a parameter three weeks ago, results improved, and you cannot reconstruct what you changed or why. The iteration log (prompts/iterations/<instrument>.yml) is that lab notebook. Every pipeline run records: instrument version, model, metrics, your interpretation, your decision, and a git hash linking to the exact file state. Reconstruct any past result with git show <hash>:<instrument_file>.


The documentation lifecycle
Implementation discoveries feed back into the research design through a structured cycle:
docs/strategy.md (human intent)
    ↓ implementation work
docs/deltas.md (bottom-up discoveries via /doc-sync)
    ↓ accumulation trigger (3+ entries or stage gate)
/strategy-sync (adversarial reconciliation)
    ↓ disposition: incorporate / acknowledge / defer
docs/strategy.md (updated) + audit trail (rationale recorded)
The strategy document stays alive rather than becoming a fossil from week one.


Exploration vs. production runs
Not all pipeline runs are equal:

Exploration runs use cheap models to test infrastructure, debug mechanics, and estimate costs. Results are not formally reportable.
Production runs use the validated model defined in your CLAUDE.md for formal evaluation. Results feed the iteration log.

The iteration log marks each run’s model and provider. Mixing providers across iterations invalidates stage comparability.


How to populate docs/strategy.md
Describe your project to Claude in plain English. Ask Claude to draft a structured plan following the section headers in docs/strategy.md. Review it carefully — the draft will be wrong in important ways, and correcting it is the most valuable intellectual work you can do at the start of the project. Your corrections encode your understanding of the problem, your methodological choices, and the constraints that make those choices non-obvious.


The 10 workflow conventions
These rules prevent recurring friction patterns. Each exists because someone learned the hard way.

Plan-first mode. When asked to diagnose or investigate, Claude presents findings and waits. No implementation unless explicitly told to. Why: prevents fixing things before you understand the problem.
Root cause first. Identify the cause before proposing a fix. Do not patch symptoms. Why: a bad instrument example causes a test failure. Improving the test hides the problem.
Model ID validation. Verify model IDs against the list in CLAUDE.md before writing them. Why: stale IDs cause silent pipeline failures.
Prefer existing files. Search before creating. Why: duplicate implementations create maintenance burden.
No autonomous API calls. Claude never runs API-calling targets without human approval. Why: API calls cost money and change pipeline state.
Commit before pipeline runs. No uncommitted changes to instrument files or pipeline functions. Why: the iteration log stores git hashes. Uncommitted changes break reproducibility.
One change at a time. One instrument component per iteration. Why: if three things change and a metric improves, you cannot tell which change helped.
Pipeline data validation. Verify result shape after a run before proceeding. Why: catches silent data issues before they propagate.
Quarto render safety. Render specific files, never the full project. Why: prevents accidentally re-rendering expensive notebooks.
Strategy reconciliation. Run /strategy-sync at stage gates or when 3+ unresolved deltas accumulate. Why: implementation drift is inevitable. Periodic reconciliation forces you to justify decisions on the record.

title: Working with Claude Code in This Project
text:
Part 4: Reference

Key file locations



Path
Purpose




COMPANION.md
Permanent principles and architecture (Tier 2)


CLAUDE.md
Project-specific instructions for Claude Code (Tier 1)


docs/strategy.md
Authoritative research methodology (Tier 2)


docs/deltas.md
Bottom-up implementation discoveries


prompts/iterations/<instrument>.yml
Iteration log per instrument


.claude/agents/
Agent configurations


.claude/skills/
Skill definitions


reports/
Dated progress reports





Skill quick reference







Command
When to use




/setup-project
Once, when starting from template


/pre-flight
Before any API-calling pipeline run


/review-iteration
After a pipeline stage, before deciding what to change


/log-iteration
After discussing results, to record the iteration


/doc-sync
After significant implementation work


/strategy-sync
At stage gates or when 3+ deltas accumulate


/progress-report
When preparing updates for external stakeholders





The iteration log schema
- iteration: 1
  instrument: "<instrument_id>"
  instrument_version: "0.1.0"
  date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
  git_commit: "0ecc0db"
  model: "<model_id>"
  provider: "anthropic"
  stage: "s1"
  changes: >
    What changed in the instrument definition since last iteration.
  results:
    overall_pass: false
    metrics:
      - test: "I_legal_outputs"    # S1 format uses test:
        value: 1.0000
        threshold: 1.0000
        pass: true
      - metric: "primary_metric"  # S2/S3 format uses metric:
        value: 0.0000
        target: 0.0000
        pass: false
  interpretation: >
    What these results mean. Written by the human.
  decision: >
    What to do next. Decided by the human.
Every past instrument version is recoverable: git show <hash>:prompts/<instrument_file>.

phase_b_pending <- FALSE

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Framing
C1 v0.7.0 extracts a measure_name string per chunk × measure, but the same fiscal act surfaces under many different forms (“ERTA”, “Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981”, “Economic Recovery Tax Act (ERTA)”, “the 1981 tax cut”). Downstream consumers — C2b motivation, deployment summaries, and any cross-country comparison — need a stable canonical mapping at the act level. The job of a “C0” Act Aggregator is to produce that mapping.
The Malaysia EN/BM consistency notebook (malay_consistency.qmd) showed that JW single-linkage at threshold 0.15 fragments acts (especially BM-side, where “Subsidi Bahan Api” + “Subsidi Gula” split off from a single budget envelope), and that this fragmentation propagates downstream: C2b reads asymmetric evidence on the two language sides and apparent disagreement is partly clustering noise. So before designing a C0 codebook we need empirical evidence on which aggregation strategy holds up.
This notebook compares five methods on a label-anchored evaluation set (US C1 outputs joined to Romer & Romer’s 40 gold-standard acts) and an out-of-distribution stress test (Malaysia EN/BM paired pairs).
Scope. Corpus-wide per country (one canonical list across all years of a corpus), not per-document and not cross-country. The C1 deployment runs per-country; C0 will too.
Asymmetric failure cost. Over-merging (collapse two real acts into one cluster) corrupts C2b inputs — bad. Under-merging (one act stays split across two clusters) just produces parallel-but-correct classifications — recoverable downstream. The headline pick should weight precision over recall.
From asymmetric cost to coverage + spurious rate. The operational form of the asymmetric-cost argument depends on what gold the eval scores against. Earlier drafts of this notebook used pairwise Precision/Recall against c0_eval_gold_pairs (the chunk-tier-tagged pool described below), with the reading that pairwise precision was costly to lose and pairwise recall recoverable. The current draft scores against Romer & Romer’s 49-act list in us_shocks.csv — independent of any pipeline output. The asymmetry translates cleanly: low spurious rate (few clusters that match no RR act) takes the place of high precision; high coverage (many RR acts hit by some cluster) takes the place of high recall; low fragmentation (few clusters per matched RR act) is the new precision-flavoured tiebreak. §“Why we pivoted away from pairwise P/R” below documents the structural ceiling that forced the change.
Gold partition caveat. The new framework scores against R&R’s act list as if it were ground truth. If R&R’s partition splits something a domain expert would have merged (or vice versa), the metric labels inherit that. The chunk-tier leakage caveat that applied to the old pairwise eval no longer applies (the RR-aligned framework bypasses chunk-tier tagging entirely), but the act-list correctness assumption is the same.
What “win” means. High coverage with low spurious rate, low fragmentation index, and high year alignment (most name-matched clusters also satisfy a ±2-year window around the act’s signing year). Metrics reported under two complementary name gates side by side — keyword containment (precision-friendly, immune to name-rewriting) and JW-min ≤ 0.30 (continuous, sensitive to abbreviations). The gap between the two surfaces methods that emit RR-style canonical names without doing more aggregation work. Bootstrap 95% CIs from resampling the 49 RR acts; n_boot = 1000 in the pipeline.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Descriptive baseline
Table 1 sizes the canonicalization problem at the chunk-measure level. Its five rows describe the C1 v0.7.0 output pool feeding C0:

Total FISCAL_MEASURE rows counts chunk × measure entries C1 flagged as FISCAL_MEASURE. Because v0.7.0 returns a measures[] array per chunk, one chunk can contribute multiple rows.
Distinct measure_name strings (any rank) counts unique surface forms across the full pool, including secondary measures from chunks whose primary measure was something else. This is the maximum pool size C0 would face if it had to canonicalize every name C1 ever emits.
Distinct measure_name strings (rank == 1) counts only the primary surface form per chunk. The codebook contract (c1_measure_id.yml) requires the first array element to be the most prominent measure discussed, so rank == 1 is C1’s primary pick per chunk. This is the realistic pool size for a primary-measure-only deployment, which matches the current C2b assumption.
Years spanned is the inclusive range of document years contributing chunks.
Distinct doc_id is the number of source documents represented.

The gap between rows 2 and 3 quantifies how much of the surface-form pool comes from secondary mentions. A small gap means rank-1 carries most of the variance and C0 can be evaluated on the rank-1 subset without much loss. A large gap means non-primary measures contribute substantial unique forms, and C0’s effective scope (primary-only vs all) becomes a load-bearing methodological choice. The eval set in §3 is built on rank == 1 only.


Show code
tibble::tibble(
  Metric = c("Total FISCAL_MEASURE rows",
             "Distinct measure_name strings (any rank)",
             "Distinct measure_name strings (rank == 1)",
             "Years spanned",
             "Distinct doc_id"),
  Value = c(
    sprintf("%d", nrow(c0_us_measure_pool)),
    sprintf("%d", dplyr::n_distinct(c0_us_measure_pool$measure_name)),
    sprintf("%d", c0_us_measure_pool |>
              dplyr::filter(measure_rank == 1L) |>
              dplyr::pull(measure_name) |> dplyr::n_distinct()),
    sprintf("%d-%d", min(c0_us_measure_pool$year, na.rm = TRUE),
            max(c0_us_measure_pool$year, na.rm = TRUE)),
    sprintf("%d", dplyr::n_distinct(c0_us_measure_pool$doc_id))
  )
) |> tt() |> tt_theme_report()




Table 1: Measure-name pool from c1_s2_results





    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                Metric
                Value
              
        
        
        
                
                  Total FISCAL_MEASURE rows
                  2941
                
                
                  Distinct measure_name strings (any rank)
                  1976
                
                
                  Distinct measure_name strings (rank == 1)
                  294
                
                
                  Years spanned
                  1946-2007
                
                
                  Distinct doc_id
                  129
                
        
      
    






Surface-form variance per gold act — the magnitude of the canonicalization problem. For each gold act_name with at least one chunk in c0_eval_gold_pairs, count the distinct C1 surface forms (rank=1 only) that map to it.


Show code
variance_per_act <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |>
  dplyr::distinct(measure_name, gold_act_name) |>
  dplyr::count(gold_act_name, name = "n_surface_forms") |>
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(n_surface_forms))

variance_per_act |>
  utils::head(25) |>
  dplyr::mutate(gold_act_name = forcats::fct_reorder(
    stringr::str_trunc(gold_act_name, 60), n_surface_forms)) |>
  ggplot(aes(n_surface_forms, gold_act_name)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#4477AA") +
  geom_text(aes(label = n_surface_forms), hjust = -0.2, size = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))) +
  labs(x = "Distinct rank-1 surface forms", y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10)








Figure 1: Distinct C1 rank-1 surface forms per gold act (top 25 most fragmented)





Table 2 drills into the same data as Figure 1, using the full any-rank measure pool (rather than the rank-1 subset used by the figure). Gold acts appear as group headers most-fragmented first, annotated with the total number of distinct C1 surface forms that ever appeared in chunks tagged with that act at any rank. Under each group, the top five most-frequently-emitted surface forms with the count of chunk-rows in which C1 emitted them. The shape of the within-group distribution is what C0 has to recover: a dominant first form with a long tail rewards conservative merging because the dominant form is already a serviceable canonical; a flat distribution needs more aggressive merging to collapse parallel forms.


Show code
chunk_to_gold <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |>
  dplyr::distinct(chunk_id, gold_act_name)

any_rank_pool <- c0_us_measure_pool |>
  dplyr::inner_join(chunk_to_gold, by = "chunk_id",
                    relationship = "many-to-many")

top5_per_act <- any_rank_pool |>
  dplyr::count(gold_act_name, measure_name, name = "n_appearances") |>
  dplyr::group_by(gold_act_name) |>
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(n_appearances), measure_name, .by_group = TRUE) |>
  dplyr::slice_head(n = 5L) |>
  dplyr::ungroup()

act_summary_anyrank <- any_rank_pool |>
  dplyr::group_by(gold_act_name) |>
  dplyr::summarize(
    n_surface_forms = dplyr::n_distinct(measure_name),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(n_surface_forms), gold_act_name)

# Interleave group-header rows (no count) with indented data rows so we get
# precise control over bolding and indentation.
indent <- strrep(" ", 4L)
rows <- list()
for (i in seq_len(nrow(act_summary_anyrank))) {
  act     <- act_summary_anyrank$gold_act_name[i]
  n_forms <- act_summary_anyrank$n_surface_forms[i]
  forms   <- top5_per_act |> dplyr::filter(gold_act_name == act)

  rows[[length(rows) + 1L]] <- tibble::tibble(
    `Surface form` = sprintf("%s (%d surface forms)", act, n_forms),
    Appearances   = ""
  )
  rows[[length(rows) + 1L]] <- tibble::tibble(
    `Surface form` = paste0(indent, forms$measure_name),
    Appearances   = as.character(forms$n_appearances)
  )
}
display_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(rows)

header_positions <- which(display_df$Appearances == "")

display_df |>
  tt() |>
  tinytable::format_tt(escape = TRUE) |>
  tt_theme_report() |>
  tinytable::style_tt(i = header_positions, bold = TRUE) |>
  tinytable::style_tt(j = 1, align = "l") |>
  tinytable::style_tt(j = 2, align = "r")

The headline: even with R&R’s well-known and well-named acts, the median act surfaces under multiple forms; the tail is heavy. A C0 that returns one cluster per surface form (i.e., no merging) is useless; a C0 that merges everything is also useless. The methods below try to land somewhere useful.

Within-document name variance: 1980 cross-section
Figure 2 sizes the data reduction happening upstream of the heatmaps below. Stage 1 counts every chunk C1 reviewed in each 1980 document (the input to FISCAL_MEASURE classification), split into the “relevant” chunks where C1 emitted at least one measure and the irrelevant chunks where it did not. Stage 2 keeps only the relevant subset — the same blue block from stage 1 redrawn on its own to make the funnel mouth visible. Stage 3 unfolds the relevant chunks into individual measure occurrences (one row per chunk × measure_rank pair), arranged by rank with the rank-1 layer marked in red. The shape of the funnel is the headline: Budget and Treasury chunks emit ≈ 5-10 measures each, so the rank-1 red band is a small slice of stage 3 — and that red slice is the pool the JW and embedding heatmaps below operate on. Rank ≥ 2 layers (grey) are out of scope for the heatmaps but are not noise; they record secondary measures C1 surfaced in the same chunk.


Show code
us_1980_docs <- c0_us_measure_pool |>
  dplyr::filter(year == 1980L) |>
  dplyr::distinct(doc_id) |>
  dplyr::mutate(series = sub("-[0-9]+$", "", doc_id)) |>
  dplyr::arrange(factor(series, levels = c("ERP", "BUDGET", "AR_TREASURY"))) |>
  dplyr::pull(doc_id)

build_funnel_df <- function(c1_s2_results, target_docs) {
  base <- c1_s2_results |>
    dplyr::filter(doc_id %in% target_docs)

  chunk_status <- base |>
    dplyr::group_by(doc_id, chunk_id) |>
    dplyr::summarize(has_fm = any(pred_label == "FISCAL_MEASURE"),
                     .groups = "drop")

  s1 <- chunk_status |>
    dplyr::count(doc_id, has_fm, name = "n") |>
    dplyr::transmute(doc_id, stage = "1. Chunks\nreviewed",
                     segment = ifelse(has_fm, "Relevant chunk",
                                              "Irrelevant chunk"), n)

  s2 <- chunk_status |>
    dplyr::filter(has_fm) |>
    dplyr::count(doc_id, name = "n") |>
    dplyr::transmute(doc_id, stage = "2. Relevant\nchunks",
                     segment = "Relevant chunk", n)

  s3 <- base |>
    dplyr::filter(pred_label == "FISCAL_MEASURE", !is.na(measure_name)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(rank_group = dplyr::case_when(
      measure_rank == 1L ~ "Rank 1",
      measure_rank == 2L ~ "Rank 2",
      measure_rank == 3L ~ "Rank 3",
      TRUE               ~ "Rank 4+"
    )) |>
    dplyr::count(doc_id, rank_group, name = "n") |>
    dplyr::transmute(doc_id, stage = "3. Surfaced\nname occurrences",
                     segment = rank_group, n)

  dplyr::bind_rows(s1, s2, s3) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      stage = factor(stage, levels = c("1. Chunks\nreviewed",
                                       "2. Relevant\nchunks",
                                       "3. Surfaced\nname occurrences")),
      doc_id = factor(doc_id, levels = target_docs),
      segment = factor(segment, levels = c("Rank 1", "Rank 2", "Rank 3",
                                           "Rank 4+", "Relevant chunk",
                                           "Irrelevant chunk"))
    )
}

funnel_df <- build_funnel_df(c1_s2_results, us_1980_docs)

funnel_palette <- c(
  "Rank 1"           = "#b2182b",
  "Rank 2"           = "#737373",
  "Rank 3"           = "#969696",
  "Rank 4+"          = "#bdbdbd",
  "Relevant chunk"   = "#4477AA",
  "Irrelevant chunk" = "#cccccc"
)

ggplot(funnel_df, aes(x = stage, y = n, fill = segment)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, position = position_stack(reverse = TRUE)) +
  geom_text(aes(label = n),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5, reverse = TRUE),
            colour = "white", size = 3, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = funnel_palette, name = NULL,
                    breaks = c("Relevant chunk", "Irrelevant chunk",
                               "Rank 1", "Rank 2", "Rank 3", "Rank 4+")) +
  facet_wrap(~ doc_id, scales = "free_y", ncol = 1) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor    = element_blank(),
        legend.position     = "right",
        strip.text          = element_text(face = "bold"))








Figure 2: Per-document funnel from C1 input to the rank-1 layer the heatmaps below operate on. Stage 1: chunks C1 reviewed, split into relevant (FISCAL_MEASURE emitted) and irrelevant. Stage 2: the relevant subset alone. Stage 3: all FISCAL_MEASURE occurrences (one per chunk × measure_rank pair) stacked by rank, with rank-1 marked in red. Stages 1-2 are counted in chunks; stage 3 in occurrences (so a single relevant chunk producing five measures contributes 1 to stage 2 and 5 to stage 3). The y-axis is free per facet to keep ERP-1980’s small counts legible alongside Treasury’s larger ones.





Figure 3 zooms into the rank-1 layer of Figure 2: it shows the pairwise Jaro-Winkler distance matrix among those rank-1 C1 measure names within each document (mirroring the Methods 2 + 3 pool used in Figure 4, so the two heatmaps carry the same acts). The heatmap pool is the within-doc surface forms intersected with the corpus-wide rank-1 pool (rownames(c0_us_embeddings)) — slightly larger than stage 3’s red layer because it includes a few names that are at rank ≥ 2 in this document but were rank-1 in some other document, while excluding rank-1 occurrences whose surface form is shared with another (so the same name shown once). Cells in the bottom band (JW < 0.15) would merge under single-linkage at the production threshold. Cells in the middle band (0.15-0.30) are near-misses — same measure with verbose-vs-bare phrasing, swapped word order, or abbreviation expansion. Cells in the top band (≥ 0.30) are genuinely distinct measures. Read each heatmap together with its numbered measure list (printed to its right). Off-diagonal cells just above the 0.15 line that link semantically-identical names are the failure cases that drive over-fragmentation; cells far above 0.30 linking surface forms that are the same act are the gap JW cannot close on its own.

Show code
jw_band_fill_doc <- ggplot2::scale_fill_stepsn(
  colours = c("#b2182b", "#f4a582", "#92c5de", "#2166ac"),
  breaks  = c(0.15, 0.30, 0.45),
  limits  = c(0, 0.6),
  oob     = scales::squish,
  name    = "JW"
)

make_jw_doc_panel <- function(this_doc_id) {
  measures_all <- c0_us_measure_pool |>
    dplyr::filter(doc_id == this_doc_id) |>
    dplyr::distinct(measure_name) |>
    dplyr::pull(measure_name)
  measures <- intersect(measures_all, rownames(c0_us_embeddings))
  n <- length(measures)
  n_dropped <- length(measures_all) - n
  if (n < 2L) return(NULL)

  dm <- stringdist::stringdistmatrix(measures, measures, method = "jw")
  df <- tidyr::expand_grid(i = seq_len(n), j = seq_len(n)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(jw = dm[cbind(i, j)]) |>
    dplyr::filter(j >= i)

  text_size <- if (n <= 10L) 3 else if (n <= 20L) 2.4 else 1.9

  heat <- ggplot(df, aes(j, i, fill = jw)) +
    geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
    geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.2f", jw)),
              colour = ifelse(df$jw < 0.30, "white", "black"),
              size = text_size) +
    jw_band_fill_doc +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq_len(n), position = "top",
                       expand = c(0, 0)) +
    scale_y_reverse(breaks = seq_len(n), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    coord_fixed() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 9) +
    theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
          axis.text = element_text(face = "bold"))

  legend_df <- tibble::tibble(
    y = rev(seq_len(n)),
    label = sprintf("%d. %s", seq_len(n),
                    stringr::str_trunc(measures, 90))
  )
  legend <- ggplot(legend_df, aes(x = 0, y = y, label = label)) +
    geom_text(hjust = 0, size = 2.7, family = "mono") +
    scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0.5, n + 0.5), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    theme_void()

  title <- sprintf("%s — %d rank-1 C1 measure names (%d non-rank-1 omitted)",
                   this_doc_id, n, n_dropped)
  heat + legend +
    patchwork::plot_layout(widths = c(1, 1.4)) +
    patchwork::plot_annotation(title = title)
}

for (this_doc in us_1980_docs) {
  p <- make_jw_doc_panel(this_doc)
  if (!is.null(p)) print(p)
}

Figure 3: Pairwise Jaro-Winkler distance among rank-1 C1 measure names within each 1980 US document, one panel per series. Red cells (JW < 0.15) would merge under single-linkage at threshold 0.15; orange cells (0.15-0.30) are near-misses that did not merge but plausibly should have; blue cells (≥ 0.30) are distinct measures. Only the upper triangle is shown; the diagonal is JW = 0 by construction. The pool is each document’s distinct C1 surface forms intersected with the rank-1 corpus pool (rownames(c0_us_embeddings)); non-rank-1 surface forms are omitted, matching Figure 4.



Figure 3 visualised what JW sees within each of the three 1980 US documents. Figure 4 is the same structure on the same documents and the same measure-name pool, but with pairwise embedding distance in place of JW. Distances are Euclidean on the L2-normalised E5 vectors c0_us_embeddings; because the embeddings are unit-norm, Euclidean distance is a monotonic transform of cosine distance (\(d_{\text{euc}} = \sqrt{2 \cdot d_{\text{cos}}}\)), so the figure mirrors what Methods 2 + 3 actually cluster on.
The colour bands are tighter than the JW figure’s because E5 packs everything into a narrow band of the unit sphere: corpus-wide pairwise Euclidean distances sit at median 0.45 with IQR 0.42-0.48. Bands at 0.40 / 0.45 / 0.50 therefore approximate “below corpus IQR” / “within IQR” / “above IQR” / “far above IQR” — a relative-rather-than-absolute reading, in keeping with the verdict text above.

Show code
emb_band_fill_doc <- ggplot2::scale_fill_stepsn(
  colours = c("#b2182b", "#f4a582", "#92c5de", "#2166ac"),
  breaks  = c(0.40, 0.45, 0.50),
  limits  = c(0, 0.6),
  oob     = scales::squish,
  name    = "Euclidean"
)

make_emb_doc_panel <- function(this_doc_id) {
  measures_all <- c0_us_measure_pool |>
    dplyr::filter(doc_id == this_doc_id) |>
    dplyr::distinct(measure_name) |>
    dplyr::pull(measure_name)
  measures <- intersect(measures_all, rownames(c0_us_embeddings))
  n <- length(measures)
  n_dropped <- length(measures_all) - n
  if (n < 2L) return(NULL)

  M <- c0_us_embeddings[measures, , drop = FALSE]
  dm <- as.matrix(stats::dist(M, method = "euclidean"))
  df <- tidyr::expand_grid(i = seq_len(n), j = seq_len(n)) |>
    dplyr::mutate(d = dm[cbind(i, j)]) |>
    dplyr::filter(j >= i)

  text_size <- if (n <= 10L) 3 else if (n <= 20L) 2.4 else 1.9

  heat <- ggplot(df, aes(j, i, fill = d)) +
    geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
    geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.2f", d)),
              colour = ifelse(df$d < 0.45, "white", "black"),
              size = text_size) +
    emb_band_fill_doc +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq_len(n), position = "top",
                       expand = c(0, 0)) +
    scale_y_reverse(breaks = seq_len(n), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    coord_fixed() +
    labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 9) +
    theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
          axis.text = element_text(face = "bold"))

  legend_df <- tibble::tibble(
    y = rev(seq_len(n)),
    label = sprintf("%d. %s", seq_len(n),
                    stringr::str_trunc(measures, 90))
  )
  legend <- ggplot(legend_df, aes(x = 0, y = y, label = label)) +
    geom_text(hjust = 0, size = 2.7, family = "mono") +
    scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0.5, n + 0.5), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    theme_void()

  title <- sprintf("%s — %d embedded C1 measure names (%d without embedding omitted)",
                   this_doc_id, n, n_dropped)
  heat + legend +
    patchwork::plot_layout(widths = c(1, 1.4)) +
    patchwork::plot_annotation(title = title)
}

for (this_doc in us_1980_docs) {
  p <- make_emb_doc_panel(this_doc)
  if (!is.null(p)) print(p)
}
