Figure 4: Pairwise Euclidean distance among L2-normalised E5 embeddings of distinct C1 measure names within each 1980 US document, one panel per series. Bands at 0.40 / 0.45 / 0.50 correspond to corpus-wide Q25 / median / Q75. Only the upper triangle is shown; the diagonal is 0 by construction. Measure names without an embedding (i.e., never appeared at rank 1 in the corpus) are omitted.



Reading Figure 4 against Figure 3, an immediate conclusion is that imposing the Rank-1 filter as in Figure 4 does not imply losing important fiscal changes. Indeed, doing so, refines the surfaced names and are more in tune with the acts Romer and Romer (n.d.) identify. Importantly, there is little within-document-within act name variance and it could be recupperated both with JW distance and with embeddings.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Tier-tagged eval set (descriptive)
This section documents c0_eval_gold_pairs, the chunk-tier-tagged pool used by the embedding-geometry probes in §“Embedding-geometry probe (F16 vs FP32)” and §“Eval-set noise probe”. It is not the headline gold for method comparison — that role belongs to R&R’s 49-act list, evaluated under the framework defined in §“Evaluation against RR’s act list” below. The structural reason for the split is in §“Why we pivoted away from pairwise P/R”, which follows immediately.
The eval set projects the chunk-tier system through C1’s LLM output along the chain aligned_data → c1_chunk_data → c1_s2_results → c0_eval_gold_pairs:

c1_chunk_data (built by R/identify_chunk_tiers.R) emits (chunk_id, doc_id, act_name, tier) rows by string-matching gold passages and act-name subcomponents against chunk text. Tier 1 marks chunks containing a verbatim gold passage; Tier 2 marks chunks matching an act name via subcomponent decomposition or co-occurrence rules. Chunks matching multiple acts within the same tier are row-replicated, one row per (chunk × act). The Tier 1 / Tier 2 exclusion is chunk-level, not (chunk, act)-level: any chunk that is Tier 1 for some act is barred from Tier 2 consideration for every act, so Tier 2 evidence on mixed-tier multi-act chunks is dropped at the source.
c1_s2_results runs the C1 LLM on these tagged chunks via leave-one-act-out cross-validation; each (chunk × gold act) pair is its own fold with its own few-shot set. The LLM’s emitted measures[] array is exploded into one row per (chunk × gold act × measure_rank), so a chunk tagged with two gold acts is classified twice and may carry different measure_name strings under each gold tag.
build_c0_eval_gold_pairs() (in R/c0_aggregator.R) keeps only rows with measure_rank == 1, pred_label == FISCAL_MEASURE, and tier ∈ {1, 2}; renames act_name → gold_act_name; and flags surface forms paired with >1 distinct gold act as ambiguous for the evaluator to majority-vote.

Two consequences carry into every downstream metric. (i) The rank-1 filter discards non-primary measures the LLM emitted, so a chunk discussing N gold acts contributes at most N rows with only the rank-1 surface form for each. (ii) Chunks the LLM mislabeled NOT_FISCAL_MEASURE drop out entirely, silently truncating multi-act representation whenever the LLM’s per-fold output is inconsistent.


Show code
t1_slice    <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |> dplyr::filter(tier == 1L)
unamb_slice <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |> dplyr::filter(!ambiguous)
amb_slice   <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |> dplyr::filter(ambiguous)

n_rows_all  <- nrow(c0_eval_gold_pairs)
n_names_all <- dplyr::n_distinct(c0_eval_gold_pairs$measure_name)

fmt_pct <- function(x, total) {
  sprintf("%d (%d%%)", x, round(100 * x / total))
}

tibble::tibble(
  Slice = c(
    "Tier 1 (verbatim passage match)",
    "Tier 1 + Tier 2 (full eval pool)",
    "    ↳ unambiguous names (1 gold act per name)",
    "    ↳ ambiguous names (≥2 gold acts per name)"
  ),
  `Chunk × gold-act rows` = c(
    as.character(nrow(t1_slice)),
    as.character(n_rows_all),
    fmt_pct(nrow(unamb_slice), n_rows_all),
    fmt_pct(nrow(amb_slice),   n_rows_all)
  ),
  `Distinct surface forms` = c(
    as.character(dplyr::n_distinct(t1_slice$measure_name)),
    as.character(n_names_all),
    fmt_pct(dplyr::n_distinct(unamb_slice$measure_name), n_names_all),
    fmt_pct(dplyr::n_distinct(amb_slice$measure_name),   n_names_all)
  ),
  `Gold acts covered` = c(
    as.character(dplyr::n_distinct(t1_slice$gold_act_name)),
    as.character(dplyr::n_distinct(c0_eval_gold_pairs$gold_act_name)),
    "—",
    "—"
  )
) |>
  tt() |>
  tt_theme_report() |>
  tinytable::style_tt(j = 1,   align = "l") |>
  tinytable::style_tt(j = 2:4, align = "r")




Table 4: Eval-set composition. Rows count (measure_name, gold_act_name, chunk_id) tuples; distinct surface forms count unique measure_name strings — the population the clusterer canonicalizes. The two indented rows partition the Tier 1 + Tier 2 pool by whether each surface form maps to one (unambiguous) or more (ambiguous) gold acts. Percentages are shares of the Tier 1 + Tier 2 totals.





    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                Slice
                Chunk × gold-act rows
                Distinct surface forms
                Gold acts covered
              
        
        
        
                
                  Tier 1 (verbatim passage match)
                  60
                  54
                  30
                
                
                  Tier 1 + Tier 2 (full eval pool)
                  592
                  267
                  39
                
                
                  ↳ unambiguous names (1 gold act per name)
                  237 (40%)
                  206 (77%)
                  —
                
                
                  ↳ ambiguous names (≥2 gold acts per name)
                  355 (60%)
                  61 (23%)
                  —
                
        
      
    






On ambiguity. Roughly 60% of the Tier 1+2 rows have a measure_name that’s been paired with more than one gold_act_name across chunks. Most cases trace to Tier 2’s permissive matching: a chunk may be tagged with act X because act X is mentioned somewhere, but the most-prominent measure C1 extracts is a different act. The evaluator handles this by majority-voting per measure_name within each bootstrap resample (so the contingency table operates on unique names). Tier 1 is shown as the cleaner anchor; Tier 1+2 is shown for statistical power.
Gold-set leakage caveat. The Tier 1/Tier 2 tags themselves were assigned by fuzzy-matching the gold act_name against the chunk text, so methods that exploit surface similarity (especially JW) will look artificially strong on this evaluation. Treat the per-act recall column as a complement — methods that nail F1 but miss specific acts may be exploiting the leakage.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
section: Why we pivoted away from pairwise P/R
text:
Why we pivoted away from pairwise P/R
c0_eval_gold_pairs has a structural ceiling on pairwise recall that comes from how the pool is constructed, not from any clusterer’s performance. build_c0_eval_gold_pairs() assigns every rank-1 measure C1 extracted in a chunk to every gold act that chunk was tier-tagged with — including chunks where C1’s primary measure was a different act. The act gold_act_name becomes a bag of co-occurring rank-1 measures rather than a list of surface variants of one act. Concretely, “Tax Reform Act of 1969” picks up members {ERISA, Asset Depreciation Range (ADR), H.R. 10947, tax bill passed in December} — distinct policy items, not paraphrases. No string-embedding clusterer can or should merge those.
Even on the Tier 1 slice (verbatim-passage tag, supposedly the clean anchor), the same pattern holds. “Deficit Reduction Act of 1984” Tier-1 members include {Economic Recovery Tax Act, Job Training Partnership Act, tax proposals (marginal income tax rate reductions and depreciation reform)}. The Tier 1 filter constrains where C1 emitted its measure, not what measure it emitted.
The §UMAP investigation in the 2026-05-28 commit cycle confirmed this empirically: 16 of 35 eligible Tier 1 acts have top-1 NN same-act rate = 0 on F16 raw embeddings (20/35 under the UMAP precision-friendly winner); across the 162-cell UMAP grid, cor(% acts reconstructed intact, F1) = +0.82 while cor(separation, pairwise_recall) = −0.07 — pairwise F1 is essentially a one-variable story about how often a gold act survives HDBSCAN as a single cluster, and that variable is capped by gold-pool construction upstream of the embedding. No UMAP variant strictly dominated the no-UMAP baseline on the original pairwise eval because both hit the same ceiling. See §“Reading the UMAP results” for the full diagnostic.
The pivot. Method comparison now scores against R&R’s authoritative 49-act list in us_shocks.csv, which is independent of any pipeline output. The pairwise-P/R evaluation against c0_eval_gold_pairs is preserved for the embedding-geometry probes (where median within/between separation is the metric, not a partition recall) and the eval-set noise probe (where the temporal mismatch is the diagnostic) — but no longer drives method selection.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Evaluation against RR’s act list

Reference: what us_shocks.csv provides
c0_us_rr_acts collapses us_shocks (one row per act × quarter × measure_type) to one row per distinct act with year_signed = year(Date signed). 49 acts spanning 1945–2007. The first few rows:


Show code
tar_load(c0_us_rr_acts)
c0_us_rr_acts |>
  utils::head(5) |>
  tt() |> tt_theme_report()




Table 5: First five rows of the RR canonical-act list (c0_us_rr_acts), sorted by year_signed.





    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                act_id
                act_name
                year_signed
              
        
        
        
                
                  1
                  Revenue Act of 1945
                  1945
                
                
                  2
                  Social Security Amendments of 1947
                  1947
                
                
                  3
                  Revenue Act of 1948
                  1948
                
                
                  4
                  Revenue Act of 1950
                  1950
                
                
                  5
                  Social Security Amendments of 1950
                  1950
                
        
      
    






This list is the metric’s reference: a predicted cluster is “good” iff some member surface form (and the cluster’s representative year) matches an entry in this list under the gates below.


Matching gates
Two complementary name gates, evaluated side by side in every metric table that follows. Both combined with a year window.
Keyword containment (precision-friendly). For each RR act, decompose the act name via generate_subcomponents() (the function R/identify_chunk_tiers.R already uses for Tier 2 chunk tagging — same decomposition, no new code path) into a set of searchable terms: the full canonical name, “and”-splits, parentheticals, embedded “{X} Act of {YYYY}” patterns, Public Law numbers, action+subject phrases. The shared BROAD_EXCLUSIONS list strips overly generic fragments. A cluster keyword-matches an RR act iff any of its surface forms (after squish_for_matching() normalisation) contains any of the act’s subcomponent terms. Property: immune to name-rewriting — a method that re-emits the canonical act name as cluster representative gets no extra credit because the test runs against the cluster’s members, not its canonical.
JW-min (continuous, abbreviation-sensitive). For each (cluster, RR act) pair, compute min over cluster members of stringdist::stringdist(member, rr_act_name, method = "jw") on lowercased strings. Threshold ≤ 0.30. Property: catches near-canonical matches that keyword-containment misses (e.g. typographic variants); also catches false matches across acts whose names share rare tokens. The JW gate is the relaxed one — it rewards methods that emit RR-style canonical names because those land at JW = 0 against the act.
The gap between coverage under keyword and coverage under JW is itself diagnostic: a method whose keyword and JW coverage agree is recovering the canonical forms via aggregation; a method with much higher JW coverage than keyword coverage is mostly re-emitting canonical names without aggregating much.


Year window
Cluster representative year = median of years extracted from members’ surface forms via extract_year_from_string() (regex \b(19|20)\d{2}\b), falling back to the mode of doc_year from c0_us_measure_pool when no member name carries an extractable year. Year gate: |cluster_year - year_signed| ≤ 2. Width ±2 chosen to (i) align with the Method 3 year-blocking variants so methods that respect the year prior get credit, and (ii) absorb retrospective references whose mode doc_year lags the signing year by 1–2.
Joint match gate per name gate: name match AND year match. A cluster that name-matches an act but is 30 years off does not match.


Headline metrics
Under each name gate, four numbers per variant:

Coverage = |{RR acts with ≥1 matching cluster}| / 49. Recall-flavoured.
Spurious rate = |{clusters with no RR-act match}| / n_clusters. Precision-flavoured; this is the metric the asymmetric-cost framing weights most.
Fragmentation index = mean over matched RR acts of (# distinct clusters claiming that act). ≥ 1 by construction. Equals 1 when each matched act has exactly one cluster; grows when methods fragment acts.
Year alignment = of name-matched pairs (name gate only, ignoring the year gate), share where the year gate also passes. Tests whether name and year signals agree — low values under the JW gate indicate the JW threshold is producing cross-decade false positives.

Coverage and spurious rate are the headline pair. Fragmentation is the precision-flavoured tiebreak between methods at similar coverage. Year alignment is mostly diagnostic.


Bootstrap CI
Resample the 49 RR acts with replacement, recompute the four metrics, take the 0.025 and 0.975 percentiles. n_boot = 1000 in the pipeline (the smoke test uses 100 for speed). The bootstrap perturbs which acts the method is held accountable for, which is the noise the CI should capture (acts vary in difficulty for any given clusterer). It does not perturb the cluster set — clusters are deterministic outputs of each method × hyperparameter cell.


Worked example
Table 6 demonstrates the matching gates on one act and two contrasting clusters. The first cluster contains the canonical surface form (Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981) and matches under both gates. The second is a hypothetical singleton cluster of just ERTA: the keyword check fails (the subcomponent term is the full canonical name; “erta” does not contain “economic recovery tax act of 1981”), and the JW distance from “erta” to the canonical name is well above 0.30. An ERTA-only cluster therefore receives no credit — the method must merge ERTA with the canonical form for either gate to pass, which is exactly the aggregation behaviour C0 is meant to demonstrate.


Show code
erta_act <- "Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981"
erta_subs <- generate_subcomponents(erta_act)
member_a  <- "Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981"
member_b  <- "ERTA"

kw_a <- any(stringr::str_detect(squish_for_matching(member_a),
                                 stringr::fixed(tolower(erta_subs$term))))
kw_b <- any(stringr::str_detect(squish_for_matching(member_b),
                                 stringr::fixed(tolower(erta_subs$term))))
jw_a <- stringdist::stringdist(tolower(member_a), tolower(erta_act),
                                method = "jw")
jw_b <- stringdist::stringdist(tolower(member_b), tolower(erta_act),
                                method = "jw")

tibble::tibble(
  `Cluster member`              = c(member_a, member_b),
  `Subcomponent terms`          = c(paste(erta_subs$term, collapse = "; "),
                                    paste(erta_subs$term, collapse = "; ")),
  `Keyword check`               = c(ifelse(kw_a, "PASS", "FAIL"),
                                    ifelse(kw_b, "PASS", "FAIL")),
  `JW distance`                 = sprintf("%.2f", c(jw_a, jw_b)),
  `JW ≤ 0.30`                   = c(ifelse(jw_a <= 0.30, "PASS", "FAIL"),
                                    ifelse(jw_b <= 0.30, "PASS", "FAIL"))
) |>
  tt() |> tt_theme_report()




Table 6: Worked example: matching two hypothetical clusters against Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981 under the keyword and JW gates.





    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                Cluster member
                Subcomponent terms
                Keyword check
                JW distance
                JW ≤ 0.30
              
        
        
        
                
                  Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981
                  Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981
                  PASS
                  0.00
                  PASS
                
                
                  ERTA
                  Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981
                  FAIL
                  0.48
                  FAIL

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Method 1 — JW single-linkage
Before reading the threshold sweep, Figure 5 shows what JW is being asked to recover. Each row is a gold act with ≥2 surface forms in the eval pool; the box and jittered points show the pairwise JW distances among the surface forms tagged to that act. Vertical dashes mark the swept thresholds (0.10–0.30). An act whose distribution sits entirely left of a threshold line is fully recoverable at that threshold; an act whose distances spread past 0.30 cannot be merged without dragging in surface forms from neighbouring acts (i.e., over-merge). Ambiguous names are kept under every gold act they’re tagged to, matching the descriptive intent of the figure rather than the evaluator’s majority-vote.


Show code
within_act_jw <- function(pool) {
  pool |>
    dplyr::distinct(measure_name, gold_act_name) |>
    dplyr::group_by(gold_act_name) |>
    dplyr::filter(dplyr::n() >= 2L) |>
    dplyr::group_modify(~ {
      names <- .x$measure_name
      d <- stringdist::stringdistmatrix(names, names, method = "jw")
      tibble::tibble(jw_distance = d[upper.tri(d)])
    }) |>
    dplyr::ungroup()
}

tier1_pairs  <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |>
  dplyr::filter(tier == 1L) |>
  within_act_jw()
tier12_pairs <- within_act_jw(c0_eval_gold_pairs)

plot_within <- function(df, title) {
  df |>
    dplyr::mutate(gold_act_name = forcats::fct_reorder(
      stringr::str_trunc(gold_act_name, 50),
      jw_distance, .fun = stats::median)) |>
    ggplot(aes(jw_distance, gold_act_name)) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = c(0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30),
               linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.3) +
    geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, fill = "#4477AA", alpha = 0.3,
                 linewidth = 0.3) +
    geom_jitter(width = 0, height = 0.15, size = 0.7, alpha = 0.6,
                colour = "#4477AA") +
    scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, NA),
                       breaks = c(0, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30, 0.5)) +
    labs(title = title, x = "Within-act pairwise JW distance", y = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 9)
}

p_a <- plot_within(tier1_pairs,  "A. Tier 1 only")
p_b <- plot_within(tier12_pairs, "B. Tier 1 + Tier 2")

p_a + p_b + patchwork::plot_layout(ncol = 2)








Figure 5: Within-gold-act pairwise JW distances. Vertical dashes mark the swept thresholds. Acts with a single surface form contribute no pairs and are omitted.





What this means for Method 1. The median within-act JW distance is ~0.47 on both tiers, while the loosest threshold swept is 0.30. The typical pair of surface forms that ought to be merged is therefore geometrically out of reach for JW at any threshold the table considers; only a small minority of pairs (near-duplicates differing in spacing, punctuation, or abbreviation expansion) sit below 0.30. The implication is unavoidable: JW single-linkage cannot canonicalize this pool on its own. The threshold sweep in Table 7 below is best read as quantifying the asymmetric coverage/spurious cost given that fundamental ceiling, not as a search for a workable operating point. A method that can bridge ERTA ↔︎ Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981 — semantic, not lexical — is required, which is what Phase B is for.
Figure 6 checks the same geometry from a different angle: instead of pairwise distances among surface forms, it measures each surface form’s JW distance to its gold-act label. If C0 had the gold canonical list as an oracle and tried to map surface forms to it by JW, the swept thresholds tell us how many surface forms it would catch. The answer mirrors Figure 5: median ~0.41 on both tiers, well above 0.30. A handful of exact matches sit at 0 (surface form is the gold label string verbatim), but most surface forms are too lexically distant from their canonical to be matched without semantic reasoning.


Show code
name_to_gold_jw <- function(pool) {
  pool |>
    dplyr::distinct(measure_name, gold_act_name) |>
    dplyr::mutate(jw_distance = stringdist::stringdist(
      measure_name, gold_act_name, method = "jw"))
}

tier1_to_gold  <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |>
  dplyr::filter(tier == 1L) |>
  name_to_gold_jw()
tier12_to_gold <- name_to_gold_jw(c0_eval_gold_pairs)

plot_to_gold <- function(df, title) {
  df |>
    dplyr::mutate(gold_act_name = forcats::fct_reorder(
      stringr::str_trunc(gold_act_name, 50),
      jw_distance, .fun = stats::median)) |>
    ggplot(aes(jw_distance, gold_act_name)) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = c(0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30),
               linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.3) +
    geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, fill = "#CC6677", alpha = 0.3,
                 linewidth = 0.3) +
    geom_jitter(width = 0, height = 0.15, size = 0.7, alpha = 0.6,
                colour = "#CC6677") +
    scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, NA),
                       breaks = c(0, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30, 0.5)) +
    labs(title = title, x = "Surface-name → gold-label JW distance", y = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 9)
}

p_a <- plot_to_gold(tier1_to_gold,  "A. Tier 1 only")
p_b <- plot_to_gold(tier12_to_gold, "B. Tier 1 + Tier 2")

p_a + p_b + patchwork::plot_layout(ncol = 2)








Figure 6: Surface-name → gold-label JW distance. Same panels, threshold lines, and visual style as Figure 5; each point is one (measure_name, gold_act_name) pair.





The within-act figures pin down what JW can mechanically do; the ladders below quantify the consequences on the RR-aligned eval the §Framing committed to. Each row is one variant (threshold × year-blocking); each cell carries a 95% bootstrap CI from resampling the 49 RR acts. Column definitions are the headline four metrics defined in §“Evaluation against RR’s act list”:

Coverage — share of 49 RR acts hit by ≥1 cluster under the joint name + year ±2 gate.
Spurious — share of clusters that match no RR act under the joint gate. The precision-flavoured column the asymmetric-cost framing weights most.
Fragmentation — mean number of clusters claiming each matched RR act (≥1).
Year alignment — diagnostic; share of name-matched pairs that also pass year ±2.

Two tables — one per name gate — so the keyword-vs-JW gap is visible side by side. Coverage will typically be higher under the JW gate (it accepts more surface forms); the keyword-vs-JW gap diagnoses how much of the JW coverage is recovery-by-near-canonical-match rather than recovery-by-aggregation.


Show code
shape_jw_rr_ladder <- function(gate_name) {
  format_rr_method_ladder(c0_jw_rr_metrics, gate = gate_name) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      `JW threshold` = as.numeric(
        stringr::str_extract(variant_id, "(?<=t)\\d\\.\\d{2}")),
      blocking = dplyr::if_else(stringr::str_detect(variant_id, "yw2"),
                                "Year ±2", "Unblocked")
    ) |>
    dplyr::arrange(blocking, `JW threshold`) |>
    dplyr::select(-variant_id)
}

render_jw_rr_ladder <- function(ladder) {
  panel_starts <- ladder |>
    dplyr::mutate(.row = dplyr::row_number()) |>
    dplyr::group_by(blocking) |>
    dplyr::summarize(start = min(.row), .groups = "drop") |>
    dplyr::arrange(start)
  group_index <- stats::setNames(as.list(panel_starts$start),
                                 panel_starts$blocking)

  display <- ladder |>
    dplyr::select(-blocking) |>
    dplyr::relocate(`JW threshold`) |>
    dplyr::mutate(`JW threshold` = sprintf("%.2f", `JW threshold`))

  display |>
    tinytable::tt() |>
    tt_theme_report() |>
    tinytable::group_tt(i = group_index)
}




Show code
render_jw_rr_ladder(shape_jw_rr_ladder("keyword"))




Table 7: JW single-linkage, keyword gate. Cells: point estimate [95% bootstrap CI]. Rows: 5 thresholds × {Unblocked, Year ±2}. The keyword gate is name-rewriting-immune: a method gets credit only if a cluster member contains an RR-act subcomponent (from generate_subcomponents()).

Reading the JW results
Three patterns from the RR-aligned ladder, focused on the asymmetric-cost shortlist (low spurious, then high coverage):
First, JW’s spurious rate is structurally high. At the precision-friendly operating point (t = 0.10, year ±2), the keyword gate hits 41 of 49 RR acts but only ~70 of 1707 clusters carry a match — the remaining ~1640 are near-singletons that aggregate nothing. JW under-aggregates rather than over-aggregates, exactly as Figure 5 predicted geometrically. Tightening the threshold lowers cluster counts (and so the spurious rate) but loses coverage. Loosening to t = 0.30 collapses everything: 180 clusters total, 1 matching cluster, 5 RR acts covered. There is no operating point on the JW front that combines low spurious with high coverage.
Second, the keyword-vs-JW gap is informative but bounded. The JW gate adds ~5–15 points of coverage on top of the keyword gate at any given threshold, because JW catches lexical near-canonicals (typographic variants, abbreviations of short distance) that don’t contain the full subcomponent string. JW single-linkage as a method does not emit RR-style canonical names — it just keeps whatever surface forms it sees — so the relaxation bonus here is mostly recovery of near-duplicates, not recovery-by-renaming. The gap will be much larger under stateful methods (Method 5) that produce a canonical per cluster.
Third, year-blocking continues to help on every variant. Spurious rates drop a few points and fragmentation usually improves; coverage holds steady or slightly improves. The temporal prior remains structural across the eval pivot.


Bottom line for Method 1
JW single-linkage cannot canonicalise this measure pool on its own. The geometric ceiling from Figure 5 is binding: most of the surface-form pairs that ought to merge sit beyond JW distance 0.30, geometrically out of reach for JW at any threshold considered. The RR-aligned ladder confirms it: at every variant the spurious rate stays above 50%, fragmentation is typically 1.5–3, and even the best variant misses ~8 of 49 RR acts under the keyword gate. The gap between ERTA and Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981 is semantic, not lexical, and JW has no machinery to bridge it.
What JW remains useful for is the role its mechanics actually support: a precision-friendly blocker — a cheap pre-filter that catches near-duplicate surface forms (spacing, punctuation, abbreviation expansion) at near-zero false-positive cost. Method 4’s hybrid pipeline below uses exactly this property: JW (or a cheap analog) prunes the pair space, and a semantic verifier handles the merges that surface similarity cannot. The load-bearing canonicalisation work, though, has to come from a method that understands meaning — Methods 2–5 are the candidate replacements.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Embedding-geometry probe (F16 vs FP32)
Method 1 closed on the observation that the gap to canonicalise this pool is semantic, not lexical. The next three sections test whether multilingual embeddings can bridge it. Before reading the eval, one preliminary: the Methods 2/3 embeddings come from a community F16 port of intfloat/multilingual-e5-large-instruct (jeffh/intfloat-multilingual-e5-large-instruct:f16) running locally under Ollama. F16 compresses the reference FP32 weights and could in principle distort the embedding geometry that HDBSCAN relies on. Two cheap probes on the gold eval pool check that the port preserves the semantic structure HDBSCAN needs.
The probes run on a 2×2 grid: precision (F16 local vs FP32 reference via DeepInfra) × eval slice (full unambiguous pool vs Tier 1 only). The FP32 reference disentangles two distinct failure modes the F16-only probe couldn’t separate: “F16 quantization broke the geometry” (FP32 passes where F16 fails) vs “the model itself can’t do this task” (both precisions fail). The Tier 1 slice in turn strips Tier 2’s permissive IKA tagging, which §“Eval-set noise probe” below shows is genuinely noisy. Reading the four cells together discriminates quantization noise, model-capacity ceiling, and label noise.


Show code
dplyr::bind_rows(
  dplyr::mutate(c0_f16_quantization_probe,       Precision = "F16 (local Ollama)",  `Eval slice` = "Full pool (Tier 1 + 2)"),
  dplyr::mutate(c0_f16_quantization_probe_tier1, Precision = "F16 (local Ollama)",  `Eval slice` = "Tier 1 only"),
  dplyr::mutate(c0_fp32_reference_probe,         Precision = "FP32 (DeepInfra)",    `Eval slice` = "Full pool (Tier 1 + 2)"),
  dplyr::mutate(c0_fp32_reference_probe_tier1,   Precision = "FP32 (DeepInfra)",    `Eval slice` = "Tier 1 only")
) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    Precision,
    `Eval slice`,
    `Top-1 NN same-act rate`    = sprintf("%.3f", top1_nn_same_act_rate),
    `Median within-act cosine`  = sprintf("%.3f", median_within_act_cosine),
    `Median between-act cosine` = sprintf("%.3f", median_between_act_cosine),
    `Separation`                = sprintf("%.3f", separation),
    `Eval names`                = n_eval_names,
    `Eval acts`                 = n_eval_acts
  ) |>
  tt() |> tt_theme_report()




Table 9: Embedding-geometry probe on a 2×2 (precision × eval slice) grid. Top-1 NN same-act rate is the share of eval names whose nearest-neighbour by cosine shares a gold act (restricted to acts with ≥2 members so the metric is well-defined). Separation = median within-act cosine − median between-act cosine; positive means the embedding preserves the act-level structure. F16 rows come from the local Ollama port (jeffh/intfloat-multilingual-e5-large-instruct:f16); FP32 rows from DeepInfra-hosted intfloat/multilingual-e5-large-instruct. Same instruction prefix on both.

F16, Tier 1: separation 0.017 (floor 0.05), top-1 NN rate 0.308 (floor 0.40) — FAIL
FP32, Tier 1: separation 0.017 (floor 0.05), top-1 NN rate 0.269 (floor 0.40) — FAIL
Reading Tier 1 vs Tier 1 keeps the gold-partition noise floor constant on both rows so the F16↔︎FP32 contrast is apples-to-apples.
Verdict — embedding-model ceiling, not quantization. Both F16 and FP32 fail the same floors, so the issue is not F16 specifically — multilingual-e5-large-instruct itself does not produce the relative cosine spread the floors require on this pool. Two interpretations to weigh: (i) E5-instruct embeddings live in a tight band of the unit sphere by design, and the 0.05/0.40 floors may be inappropriate for this family; (ii) the model is genuinely under-resolving fiscal-act semantics and a stronger or differently-tuned embedding family is needed. Either way, the FP32 migration is not the fix. Read Methods 2/3 below as the empirical end-to-end test — if HDBSCAN’s Tier 1 numbers are competitive with JW, the floors were the over-strict reading.
E5-family embeddings always live in a narrow band of the unit sphere, so absolute cosines aren’t crisp — the probe targets the relative gap between within-act and between-act pairs, which is the property HDBSCAN cares about. Methods 2 and 3 below continue to read c0_us_embeddings (the F16 matrix); the FP32 numbers above are diagnostic only.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
section: Eval-set noise probe: surface-year vs doc-year mismatch
text:
Eval-set noise probe: surface-year vs doc-year mismatch
An alternative explanation for a soft F16 verdict — and a noise source the within-act geometry from §Method 1 already hinted at — is that the gold pairs themselves are temporally noisy. If C1 sometimes catches contemporary discussions of acts passed decades earlier (e.g. a 2010 ERP paragraph referring back to the Revenue Act of 1964), the resulting (measure_name, gold_act_name) pair will look lexically distant — not because the model misclassified, but because the surface form drifts as the act becomes historical context rather than current policy. Figure 7 probes this directly: for each eval tuple whose surface form contains a 4-digit year, plot the gap between the document’s publication year and the year referenced in the surface form against the JW distance to the gold label.


Show code
year_pattern <- "\\b(19|20)\\d{2}\\b"

chunk_doc_year <- c0_us_measure_pool |>
    dplyr::distinct(chunk_id, doc_year = year)

noise_diag <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |>
    dplyr::distinct(measure_name, gold_act_name, chunk_id, tier) |>
    dplyr::left_join(chunk_doc_year, by = "chunk_id") |>
    dplyr::mutate(
        surface_year = as.integer(stringr::str_extract(measure_name, year_pattern)),
        year_diff = doc_year - surface_year,
        jw_distance = stringdist::stringdist(measure_name, gold_act_name,
            method = "jw"
        ),
        tier_label = factor(tier,
            levels = c(1L, 2L),
            labels = c("Tier 1", "Tier 2")
        )
    ) |>
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(surface_year), !is.na(doc_year))

ggplot(noise_diag, aes(year_diff, jw_distance, colour = tier_label)) +
    geom_hline(
        yintercept = c(0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30),
        linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.3
    ) +
    geom_vline(
        xintercept = 0, linetype = "dotted",
        colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.3
    ) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.6) +
    geom_smooth(aes(colour = tier_label),
        se = FALSE,
       linewidth = 0.4, linetype = "dashed"
    ) +
    scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
    labs(
        x = "Doc year − surface-referenced year",
        y = "JW distance (surface name → gold label)",
        colour = NULL
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
    theme(legend.position = "top")








Figure 7: Temporal mismatch in eval pairs. Each point is one (measure_name, gold_act_name, chunk_id) tuple whose surface form contains a 4-digit year. x = doc publication year − year referenced in the surface form (large positive ⇒ retrospective reference); y = JW distance from surface form to gold label. Tuples whose surface form has no extractable year are omitted.





Figure 8 repeats the panel on the semantic axis: y is cosine distance between the surface-name and gold-label embeddings (E5-multilingual F16, L2-normalised — so cosine distance = 1 − dot product, on the same scale as the F16 probe). The interpretation flips relative to JW: if retrospective references drift only lexically (paraphrase) but stay semantically anchored, the cloud should stay flat as x grows; if they drift semantically as well (different terminology or focus when describing the act decades later), the cloud should rise. Reading the two figures together separates the two failure modes.


Show code
surface_in_emb <- intersect(noise_diag$measure_name,
                            rownames(c0_us_embeddings))
gold_in_emb    <- intersect(noise_diag$gold_act_name,
                            rownames(c0_us_gold_embeddings))

noise_diag_cos <- noise_diag |>
  dplyr::filter(measure_name %in% surface_in_emb,
                gold_act_name %in% gold_in_emb)

S <- c0_us_embeddings[noise_diag_cos$measure_name, , drop = FALSE]
G <- c0_us_gold_embeddings[noise_diag_cos$gold_act_name, , drop = FALSE]
noise_diag_cos$cos_distance <- 1 - rowSums(S * G)

ggplot(noise_diag_cos, aes(year_diff, cos_distance, colour = tier_label)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.6) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.4, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(x = "Doc year − surface-referenced year",
       y = "Cosine distance (surface name → gold label)",
       colour = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")








Figure 8: Same panel as Figure 7 but with cosine distance (E5-multilingual F16 embeddings, L2-normalised) on y. Pairs are dropped when either the surface form or the gold label is absent from its respective embedding matrix.





Finally, Figure 9 puts the two distance measures head-to-head on the full eval pool (no year filter): one point per distinct (measure_name, gold_act_name) pair, x is the JW distance, y is the cosine distance. The correlation answers whether the embedding is buying us anything JW couldn’t already give us — a strong positive correlation would mean E5 is essentially re-deriving lexical similarity, while a weak correlation means the two distance measures disagree on which pairs are similar, and there is genuine semantic signal in the embedding above and beyond surface overlap. Both Pearson (linear) and Spearman (rank) correlations are reported because the JW–cosine relationship is plausibly nonlinear.


Show code
pair_min_tier <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |>
  dplyr::group_by(measure_name, gold_act_name) |>
  dplyr::summarize(tier = min(tier, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  dplyr::mutate(tier_label = factor(tier, levels = c(1L, 2L),
                                    labels = c("Tier 1", "Tier 2")))

distance_pairs <- pair_min_tier |>
  dplyr::filter(measure_name  %in% rownames(c0_us_embeddings),
                gold_act_name %in% rownames(c0_us_gold_embeddings)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(jw_distance = stringdist::stringdist(
    measure_name, gold_act_name, method = "jw"))

S <- c0_us_embeddings[distance_pairs$measure_name, , drop = FALSE]
G <- c0_us_gold_embeddings[distance_pairs$gold_act_name, , drop = FALSE]
distance_pairs$cos_distance <- 1 - rowSums(S * G)

cor_pearson  <- stats::cor(distance_pairs$jw_distance,
                           distance_pairs$cos_distance, method = "pearson")
cor_spearman <- stats::cor(distance_pairs$jw_distance,
                           distance_pairs$cos_distance, method = "spearman")

ggplot(distance_pairs, aes(jw_distance, cos_distance, colour = tier_label)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 1.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE,
              linewidth = 0.4, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("label", x = Inf, y = -Inf,
           label = sprintf("Pearson  r = %.2f\nSpearman ρ = %.2f\nn = %d pairs",
                           cor_pearson, cor_spearman, nrow(distance_pairs)),
           hjust = 1.05, vjust = -0.2, size = 3.2,
           label.size = 0, fill = scales::alpha("white", 0.7)) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(x = "JW distance (surface name → gold label)",
       y = "Cosine distance (surface name → gold label)",
       colour = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")








Figure 9: JW distance vs cosine distance for every distinct (measure_name, gold_act_name) pair in the eval pool. Colour marks the cleanest tier in which the pair appears (Tier 1 if at least one chunk pairs the surface form with the gold act via verbatim match). Pairs whose surface form or gold label is missing from its embedding matrix are dropped. Annotated correlations include the full pool; the dashed line is an unweighted linear fit.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Method 2 — Multilingual embedding + HDBSCAN
Switching from string distance to embedding distance is what the within-act geometry from §Method 1 demanded. Method 2 embeds each measure name with intfloat/multilingual-e5-large-instruct, L2-normalises the vectors so cosine ranking equals euclidean ranking, then runs HDBSCAN over the resulting distance matrix. Three min_cluster_size values are swept — 2 (most permissive), 3, and 5 (close to the median gold-act size). Singleton “noise” points HDBSCAN refuses to merge are kept as their-own-clusters; under the RR-aligned eval they contribute to the spurious rate denominator (singleton clusters with no RR-act match raise spurious; singletons that do contain a canonical name still match).


Show code
shape_hdb_rr_ladder <- function(gate_name, blocking_filter) {
  format_rr_method_ladder(c0_hdbscan_rr_metrics, gate = gate_name) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      `min_cluster_size` = as.integer(
        stringr::str_extract(variant_id, "(?<=_m)\\d+(?=_)")),
      .blocking = dplyr::if_else(stringr::str_detect(variant_id, "yw2"),
                                 "yw2", "unblocked")
    ) |>
    dplyr::filter(.blocking == blocking_filter) |>
    dplyr::arrange(`min_cluster_size`) |>
    dplyr::select(-variant_id, -.blocking) |>
    dplyr::relocate(`min_cluster_size`)
}

render_hdb_rr_ladder <- function(ladder) {
  ladder |>
    tinytable::tt() |>
    tt_theme_report()
}




Show code
render_hdb_rr_ladder(shape_hdb_rr_ladder("keyword", "unblocked"))




Table 10: HDBSCAN unblocked, keyword gate. Cells: point estimate [95% bootstrap CI]. Rows: 3 min_cluster_size settings. Compare to Table 7 — if embeddings buy us what JW couldn’t, coverage should hold and spurious should drop.

Show code
set.seed(20260523L)
umap_xy <- uwot::umap(c0_us_embeddings,
                      n_neighbors = 15L, min_dist = 0.1,
                      metric = "cosine", verbose = FALSE)
umap_df <- tibble::tibble(
  measure_name = rownames(c0_us_embeddings),
  umap_x = umap_xy[, 1L],
  umap_y = umap_xy[, 2L]
)

best_hdb_variant_f1 <- c0_hdbscan_metrics |>
  dplyr::filter(metric == "pairwise_f1", eval_tier == "tier1",
                stringr::str_detect(variant_id, "unblocked")) |>
  dplyr::slice_max(value, n = 1L, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  dplyr::pull(variant_id)

hdb_best <- c0_hdbscan_clusters |>
  dplyr::filter(variant_id == best_hdb_variant_f1) |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    measure_name,
    cluster_label = dplyr::if_else(n_members >= 2L,
                                   sprintf("c%d", cluster_id),
                                   NA_character_)
  )

gold_unamb <- c0_eval_gold_pairs |>
  dplyr::filter(!ambiguous) |>
  dplyr::distinct(measure_name, gold_act_name)

p_pred <- umap_df |>
  dplyr::left_join(hdb_best, by = "measure_name") |>
  ggplot(aes(umap_x, umap_y, colour = cluster_label)) +
  geom_point(size = 1.4, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_colour_discrete(na.value = "grey75", guide = "none") +
  labs(title = sprintf("Predicted HDBSCAN clusters (variant %s)",
                       best_hdb_variant_f1),
       x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 9) +
  theme(axis.text = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

p_gold <- umap_df |>
  dplyr::left_join(gold_unamb, by = "measure_name") |>
  dplyr::mutate(gold_label = stringr::str_trunc(gold_act_name, 30)) |>
  ggplot(aes(umap_x, umap_y, colour = gold_label)) +
  geom_point(size = 1.4, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_colour_discrete(na.value = "grey75", guide = "none") +
  labs(title = "Gold act (unambiguous eval names)",
       x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 9) +
  theme(axis.text = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

p_pred + p_gold + patchwork::plot_layout(ncol = 2)








Figure 10: UMAP projection of E5 embeddings. Left: coloured by predicted HDBSCAN cluster (best unblocked variant by Tier 1 F1); singleton/noise names in grey. Right: coloured by gold act for names in the unambiguous eval pool; names outside the eval pool in grey. Same 2D coordinates in both panels.






Reading the HDBSCAN results
The headline comparison is Table 10 against Table 7: if embeddings buy us what JW couldn’t, HDBSCAN should hold or improve coverage while collapsing the spurious-rate gap. The UMAP scatter Figure 10 is diagnostic — predicted clusters that overlap a single gold colour on the right panel are correct merges; predicted clusters spanning multiple gold colours are over-merges (note: the gold panel uses c0_eval_gold_pairs so the colour palette inherits the gold-pool noise documented in §“Why we pivoted away from pairwise P/R” — read it as a relative-structure visualisation, not as ground truth on which clusters are correct). The §Synthesis at the bottom of the notebook applies the precision-friendly shortlist rule (lowest spurious, then highest coverage) to pick a candidate variant across methods.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Method 3 — Year-blocked variants
Method 3 reruns the same HDBSCAN sweep with the year-window mask from §Method 1: pairs whose chunk years differ by more than 2 are forced to maximum distance and so cannot merge. The prior is that the same act tends to surface in a narrow temporal window, so the mask should reduce cross-decade over-merges without sacrificing coverage.
Partial redundancy with the match gate. The RR-aligned eval already applies a year ±2 window between cluster representative year and year_signed, so a cluster that would have over-merged across decades will fail the year gate anyway and not match the wrong act. Year-blocking at clustering time and year ±2 at match time are not the same operation, though: clustering-time blocking prevents the merge from happening (the cluster doesn’t form), while match-time blocking lets the merge happen but disqualifies the resulting cluster from claiming an act of the wrong year. So year-blocking still helps by reducing the count of cross-decade clusters that would otherwise raise the spurious rate denominator. The expected pattern: spurious rate drops modestly under year-blocking; coverage and fragmentation hold.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Methods 2 + 3 with UMAP dimensionality reduction
The F16 and FP32 embedding-geometry probes above both failed the separation ≥ 0.05 and top-1 NN same-act rate ≥ 0.40 floors on the gold pool. The verdict text considered two interpretations — F16 quantization noise, and the embedding-model ceiling — but did not weigh a third: high-dimensional distance concentration. intfloat/multilingual-e5-large-instruct returns 1024-dimensional vectors, and the unblocked HDBSCAN above operates on the full-dimensional cosine distance matrix. Density-based clustering is known to degrade above k≈50 dim through hubness and farthest-to-nearest ratio compression; the standard fix in the BERTopic / Top2Vec neighbourhood is to interpose UMAP between the embedding and the clusterer, collapsing the ambient dim to ~5–10 and (optionally) packing within-cluster points tightly via min_dist = 0. This section ports that recipe into Methods 2 + 3 and reads the with-vs-without delta against the no-UMAP baseline above.
The sweep grid is the cartesian product of three UMAP hyperparameters with the existing HDBSCAN grid:

n_neighbors ∈ {5, 15, 30} — uwot default 15; the small end emphasises local structure, the large end emphasises global structure.
n_components ∈ {2, 5, 10} — uwot default 2; BERTopic’s standard is 5; 10 is the upper end of clustering practice.
min_dist ∈ {0.0, 0.01, 0.1} — uwot default 0.01; 0.0 is BERTopic’s tight-packing default; 0.1 matches the existing diagnostic UMAP plot at Figure 10.
Inner sweep matches the no-UMAP variants: min_cluster_size ∈ {2, 3, 5} and year_window ∈ {unblocked, ±2}.

That gives 27 UMAP cells × 6 HDBSCAN cells = 162 variants. Metric "cosine" is fixed (the input embeddings are L2-normalised); UMAP is reduced once per UMAP cell and reused across the inner HDBSCAN sub-cells. The seed is fixed at the target level (run_hdbscan_umap_clusters_grid(seed = 20260526L)) so the entire sweep is deterministic under tar_make(). Distance metric inside HDBSCAN switches from cosine on the raw embeddings to Euclidean on the UMAP output (UMAP does not preserve L2-normalisation, so cosine on the reduced space is not meaningful); the year-window mask uses a max(d) + 1 sentinel instead of the cosine 1.0 cap.


Show code
# Parse the four sweep dimensions out of the variant_id once so every chunk
# below can read them by name.
parse_umap_variant <- function(metrics_or_clusters_df) {
  metrics_or_clusters_df |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      umap_n_neighbors  = as.integer(stringr::str_extract(variant_id,
                                                          "(?<=nn)\\d+")),
      umap_n_components = as.integer(stringr::str_extract(variant_id,
                                                          "(?<=nc)\\d+")),
      umap_min_dist     = as.numeric(stringr::str_extract(variant_id,
                                                          "(?<=md)\\d+\\.\\d+")),
      min_cluster_size  = as.integer(stringr::str_extract(variant_id,
                                                          "(?<=_m)\\d+(?=_)")),
      blocking          = dplyr::if_else(stringr::str_detect(variant_id, "yw2"),
                                         "year ±2", "unblocked")
    )
}




Show code
# Per-(blocking, min_cluster_size) winner under the precision-friendly rule
# rewritten for the RR-aligned framework: lowest spurious rate first, then
# highest coverage. Returns variant_ids to join back against the formatted
# ladder for display.
pick_umap_rr_winners <- function(gate_name) {
  spurious_per_variant <- c0_hdbscan_umap_rr_metrics |>
    dplyr::filter(metric == "spurious_rate", gate == gate_name) |>
    dplyr::transmute(variant_id, spurious = value)
  coverage_per_variant <- c0_hdbscan_umap_rr_metrics |>
    dplyr::filter(metric == "coverage", gate == gate_name) |>
    dplyr::transmute(variant_id, coverage = value)
  spurious_per_variant |>
    dplyr::inner_join(coverage_per_variant, by = "variant_id") |>
    parse_umap_variant() |>
    dplyr::group_by(blocking, min_cluster_size) |>
    dplyr::arrange(spurious, dplyr::desc(coverage), .by_group = TRUE) |>
    dplyr::slice_head(n = 1L) |>
    dplyr::ungroup() |>
    dplyr::select(variant_id, blocking, min_cluster_size,
                  umap_n_neighbors, umap_n_components, umap_min_dist)
}

assemble_umap_rr_shortlist <- function(gate_name) {
  winners <- pick_umap_rr_winners(gate_name)
  formatted <- format_rr_method_ladder(c0_hdbscan_umap_rr_metrics,
                                       gate = gate_name)
  winners |>
    dplyr::inner_join(formatted, by = "variant_id") |>
    dplyr::arrange(blocking, min_cluster_size) |>
    dplyr::transmute(
      `Blocking`         = blocking,
      `min_cluster_size`,
      `n_neighbors`      = umap_n_neighbors,
      `n_components`     = umap_n_components,
      `min_dist`         = sprintf("%.3f", umap_min_dist),
      Coverage, Spurious, Fragmentation, `Year alignment`,
      `n clusters`
    )
}

  c0_hdbscan_umap_probe_tier1_fp32 |> dplyr::mutate(precision = "FP32 (DeepInfra)",   slice = "Tier 1 only")
) |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols      = c(top1_nn_same_act_rate, separation),
    names_to  = "metric",
    values_to = "value"
  ) |>
  dplyr::mutate(metric = dplyr::recode(metric,
    top1_nn_same_act_rate = "Top-1 NN same-act rate",
    separation            = "Separation (between − within, Euclidean)"
  )) |>
  ggplot(aes(value, fill = slice)) +
  geom_histogram(position = "identity", alpha = 0.6, bins = 15) +
  geom_vline(data = tibble::tibble(
    metric = c("Top-1 NN same-act rate",
               "Separation (between − within, Euclidean)"),
    floor  = c(0.40, 0)
  ), aes(xintercept = floor), linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  facet_grid(precision ~ metric, scales = "free_x") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Count (UMAP cells)", fill = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")








Figure 11: Embedding-geometry probe in UMAP-reduced space — F16 vs FP32, the same model precisions contrasted in Table 9 at no-UMAP. Columns facet the two probe metrics; rows facet embedding precision (F16 = local Ollama community port, FP32 = DeepInfra-hosted reference). The F16 panels (top row) reproduce the earlier UMAP read; the FP32 panels (bottom row) add the missing precision contrast in UMAP space. Each bar counts UMAP cells (out of 27) whose probe value falls in the bin; fill marks eval slice (Full pool = Tier 1 + 2 unambiguous, Tier 1 = verbatim-passage matches only). Dashed lines are the F16/FP32 floors carried over from Table 9: 0.40 for top-1 NN; 0 for separation (Euclidean — the 0.05 cosine floor at Table 9 does not translate). Identical UMAP grid, seed (20260526), and probe logic across the four panels; the only thing that varies between F16 and FP32 rows is the input embedding matrix.






Reading the UMAP results
The headline reading is empirical: across the 162 UMAP variants in c0_hdbscan_umap_rr_metrics, no UMAP variant strictly dominates the no-UMAP HDBSCAN baseline on the coverage/spurious front under either gate. The shortlist tables above pick the best UMAP cell per (min_cluster_size × year-blocking) sub-cell by the asymmetric-cost rule (lowest spurious, then highest coverage); the picks land at comparable spurious rates and comparable coverage to the corresponding no-UMAP rows in Table 10 and Table 12. UMAP-PF (the precision-friendly UMAP winner overall) does cut the singleton share in the cluster-count distribution from 38% (no-UMAP baseline hdb_m2_yw2) to 14%, but most of the singletons it removes are noise points that wouldn’t have matched any RR act anyway. Two independent diagnostics explain why the front doesn’t move.
First, UMAP improves the aggregate embedding geometry but not the neighbour identity HDBSCAN and the name gates actually use. Figure 11 shows within-vs-between separation moves dramatically — F16 raw 0.005 → UMAP median 0.379, a ≈70× lift, with positive separation in 27/27 F16 cells and 26/27 FP32 cells. Top-1 NN same-act rate barely moves: F16 raw 0.261 → UMAP cells span [0.18, 0.31], and zero of 108 probe cells (54 F16 + 54 FP32 across both eval slices) clear the 0.40 floor. The F16 ↔︎ FP32 contrast on top-1 NN is flat, which resolves the verdict left open by the no-UMAP F16/FP32 probe in §“Embedding-geometry probe”: the bottleneck is the embedding model, not F16 quantization. UMAP cleans aggregate geometry without changing the literal nearest neighbour, and the literal NN is what HDBSCAN’s density-based clustering and the RR-aligned name gates both depend on.
Second, the upstream gold-pool ceiling documented in §“Why we pivoted away from pairwise P/R” still binds the RR-aligned eval, just differently. The new framework removes the chunk-tier act_name aggregation noise that capped pairwise recall against c0_eval_gold_pairs, but coverage against R&R’s 49-act list is still capped by whether HDBSCAN finds ≥1 surface form per act that carries enough of the canonical name to satisfy the gates. Tiny clusters that fragment off the abbreviated forms (ERTA, P.L. 97-34) without merging back to the canonical fail both gates regardless of UMAP arrangement. The earlier UMAP draft’s third hypothesis (that n_components would be the load-bearing UMAP knob) remains empirically wrong on the RR-aligned eval too: no UMAP knob moves the front meaningfully because the front is not where UMAP’s lever points. BERTopic-style UMAP-before-density-clustering is therefore not the load-bearing C0 architecture.
Implications for Method 4. The same diagnosis predicts the hybrid LLM pairwise judge will break through some of the gap but not all of it. Genuine surface variants (ERTA ↔︎ Economic Recovery Tax Act of 1981 ↔︎ P.L. 97-34) that HDBSCAN scattered by 1024-D cosine-distance concentration will resolve once the judge sees the candidate pairs — top-k embedding retrieval should surface them as candidates even at F16 (top-1 may miss but top-5/10 will catch). The judge cannot manufacture canonical names from scratch, though, so methods that emit canonicals (Method 5) will look better on the keyword gate than methods that only verify pairs (Method 4); the keyword-vs-JW gap in the §Synthesis ladder will surface that distinction.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Method comparison ladder


Show code
# Asymmetric-cost picker: lowest spurious_rate, then highest coverage,
# under the requested name gate. Numeric sort, not lexicographic on the
# formatted ladder cells.
pick_best_by_rr <- function(metrics_long, gate_name) {
  sp <- metrics_long |>
    dplyr::filter(metric == "spurious_rate", gate == gate_name) |>
    dplyr::transmute(variant_id, spurious = value)
  cv <- metrics_long |>
    dplyr::filter(metric == "coverage", gate == gate_name) |>
    dplyr::transmute(variant_id, coverage = value)
  sp |>
    dplyr::inner_join(cv, by = "variant_id") |>
    dplyr::arrange(spurious, dplyr::desc(coverage)) |>
    dplyr::slice_head(n = 1L) |>
    dplyr::pull(variant_id)
}

build_method_comparison <- function(gate_name) {
  best_jw       <- pick_best_by_rr(c0_jw_rr_metrics, gate_name)
  best_hdb      <- pick_best_by_rr(c0_hdbscan_rr_metrics, gate_name)
  best_hdb_umap <- pick_best_by_rr(c0_hdbscan_umap_rr_metrics, gate_name)

  jw_row <- format_rr_method_ladder(c0_jw_rr_metrics, gate = gate_name) |>
    dplyr::filter(variant_id == best_jw) |>
    dplyr::mutate(Method = "JW (best variant)")
  hdb_row <- format_rr_method_ladder(c0_hdbscan_rr_metrics,
                                     gate = gate_name) |>
    dplyr::filter(variant_id == best_hdb) |>
    dplyr::mutate(Method = "HDBSCAN (best variant)")
  hdb_umap_row <- format_rr_method_ladder(c0_hdbscan_umap_rr_metrics,
                                          gate = gate_name) |>
    dplyr::filter(variant_id == best_hdb_umap) |>
    dplyr::mutate(Method = "HDBSCAN + UMAP (best variant)")

  dplyr::bind_rows(jw_row, hdb_row, hdb_umap_row) |>
    dplyr::rename(Variant = variant_id) |>
    dplyr::relocate(Method, Variant)
}

Out-of-distribution: Malaysia EN/BM






WarningPhase B pending



Once Phase B is built, the best variant from each method is applied to the Malaysia EN+BM measure pool (malay_er_c1_measures); we report the fraction of malay_er_curated_matches EN↔︎BM pairs that land in the same predicted cluster. Methods that rely on surface similarity (JW) are expected to fail here because the language families are different; multilingual embedding methods should hold up.

title: C0 Act Aggregator — Method Comparison
text:
Synthesis






NoteMethods 4 + 5 still pending



A final method + parameter recommendation waits for all five methods to be evaluated. Methods 1–3 (JW, HDBSCAN unblocked, HDBSCAN year-blocked) are now wired against the RR-aligned eval; Methods 4 (hybrid LLM judge) and 5 (stateful canonical builder) remain to land.
Until then, the shortlist below applies the precision-friendly rule from §Framing to each finished method in isolation: among variants with the lowest spurious rate, pick the one with the highest coverage. Compare the JW pick against the HDBSCAN pick to see whether the semantic bridge embeddings provide outweighs whatever lexical-only precision JW retains.




Show code
# Top-N variants under (spurious asc, coverage desc) for a given metrics
# tibble and gate. Returns a wide tibble carrying numeric `spurious_num` and
# `coverage_num` keys so downstream callers can re-sort across multiple
# shortlists (e.g. when union-ing HDBSCAN and HDBSCAN+UMAP) before dropping
# the numeric keys for display via finalize_rr_shortlist().
build_rr_shortlist <- function(metrics_long, gate_name, top_n = 5L) {
  sp <- metrics_long |>
    dplyr::filter(metric == "spurious_rate", gate == gate_name) |>
    dplyr::transmute(variant_id, spurious_num = value,
                     sp_lo = ci_lo, sp_hi = ci_hi,
                     n_clusters, n_clusters_matched, n_rr_acts_matched)
  cv <- metrics_long |>
    dplyr::filter(metric == "coverage", gate == gate_name) |>
    dplyr::transmute(variant_id, coverage_num = value,
                     cv_lo = ci_lo, cv_hi = ci_hi)
  sp |>
    dplyr::inner_join(cv, by = "variant_id") |>
    dplyr::arrange(spurious_num, dplyr::desc(coverage_num)) |>
    utils::head(top_n) |>
    dplyr::transmute(
      variant_id,
      spurious_num, coverage_num,
      Spurious           = sprintf("%.3f [%.3f, %.3f]",
                                    spurious_num, sp_lo, sp_hi),
      Coverage           = sprintf("%.3f [%.3f, %.3f]",
                                    coverage_num, cv_lo, cv_hi),
      `n clusters`       = n_clusters,
      `Clusters matched` = n_clusters_matched,
      `RR acts matched`  = n_rr_acts_matched
    )
}

finalize_rr_shortlist <- function(shortlist) {
  shortlist |> dplyr::select(-spurious_num, -coverage_num)
}

If a UMAP+HDBSCAN variant displaces no-UMAP HDBSCAN at the top, deploy with UMAP for Malaysia. If JW dominates spurious but trails coverage, JW is the natural blocker candidate for Method 4 — the hybrid pipeline reconciles the spurious-vs-coverage trade by routing JW’s precision-friendly candidate pairs through a semantic verifier.

title: Training Data Overview
text:
This document provides a comprehensive overview of the training data pipeline for Phase 0, documenting how raw source data (us_labels, us_shocks, us_body) is transformed into evaluation-ready datasets for the C1-C4 codebook framework.
Purpose:

Transparently describe all data transformations
Document observation-level changes (passage to act, document to chunk, etc.)
Explain key assumptions and design decisions
Visualize data flow and composition at each stage

Key Transformation:
The most significant transformation is from passage-level (us_labels with 340 passages describing 44 unique acts) to act-level (aligned_data with 44 rows, one per act). Multiple passages describing the same act are grouped and concatenated into a single observation.


Show code
library(targets)
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(here)

here::i_am("notebooks/data_overview.qmd")
tar_config_set(store = here("_targets"))
source(here("R/gt_theme.R"))
set_theme(theme_minimal())

# Source data
us_labels <- tar_read(us_labels)
us_shocks <- tar_read(us_shocks)
us_body <- tar_read(us_body)

# Derived data
aligned_data <- tar_read(aligned_data)

# C1 pipeline
chunks <- tar_read(chunks)
c1_chunk_data <- tar_read(c1_chunk_data)

title: Training Data Overview
text:
Data Composition and Transformation Pipeline
This section documents how the raw source data (us_labels, us_shocks, us_body) is transformed into evaluation-ready datasets. We describe the transformation logic, observation-level changes, key assumptions, and provide visual summaries at each stage.

Data Flow Overview
The data flows through multiple transformation stages, changing observation levels and structure:








Data Transformation Pipeline Overview


Observation levels and row counts at each stage


Dataset
Unit of Observation
Row Count
Unique Acts
Purpose




us_labels
Passage (multiple per act)
376
40
Human-labeled passages describing acts


us_shocks
Quarter (multiple per act)
90
49
Romer & Romer shock magnitudes/timing


us_body
Document (one per year-body)
360
—
Extracted text from government PDFs


aligned_data
Act (one row per act)
39
39
Joined labels + shocks at act level


chunks
Chunk (sliding window over pages)
15,061
—
Sliding-window document chunks for LLM input


c1_chunk_data
Tiered chunks (list: tier1, tier2, negatives)
13,688
39
Tier-assigned chunks for C1 LOOCV evaluation







Key Transformation:
The most significant transformation is from passage-level (us_labels) to act-level (aligned_data). Multiple passages describing the same act are grouped and concatenated into a single observation.


Component 1: Source Data (us_labels)
Observation Level: Passage (multiple rows per act)
Structure: Human-labeled passages from Romer & Romer (2010) dataset describing motivations for US fiscal policy acts.


Show code
# Show structure
us_labels_structure <- us_labels %>%
  summarize(
    n_passages = n(),
    n_unique_acts = n_distinct(act_name),
    passages_per_act_min = min(table(act_name)),
    passages_per_act_median = median(table(act_name)),
    passages_per_act_max = max(table(act_name)),
    n_categories = n_distinct(category),
    date_range = paste(min(date, na.rm = TRUE), "to", max(date, na.rm = TRUE))
  )

tibble(
  Metric = c(
    "Total passages",
    "Unique acts",
    "Passages per act (min/median/max)",
    "Motivation categories",
    "Date range"
  ),
  Value = c(
    scales::comma(us_labels_structure$n_passages),
    as.character(us_labels_structure$n_unique_acts),
    sprintf("%d / %d / %d",
            us_labels_structure$passages_per_act_min,
            us_labels_structure$passages_per_act_median,
            us_labels_structure$passages_per_act_max),
    as.character(us_labels_structure$n_categories),
    us_labels_structure$date_range
  )
) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(title = "us_labels: Passage-Level Structure") %>%
  cols_label(Metric = "Metric", Value = "Value") %>%
  gt_theme_report()








us_labels: Passage-Level Structure


Metric
Value




Total passages
376

2 / 8 / 26

1945-01-03 to 2003-01-11







Show code

us_labels %>%
  count(act_name) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = n)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 1, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Passages per Act (us_labels)",
    x = "Number of Passages",
    y = "Number of Acts"

us_labels %>%
  count(category, sort = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(title = "Passage Distribution by Category (us_labels)") %>%
  cols_label(category = "Motivation Category", n = "Passages", pct = "Percent") %>%
  fmt_number(columns = pct, decimals = 1, pattern = "{x}%") %>%

Example Passages (Act with Multiple Passages):

Show code
# Show example act with multiple passages
example_act <- us_labels %>%
  group_by(act_name) %>%
  filter(n() >= 3) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(act_name == first(act_name)) %>%
  arrange(act_name)

cat(sprintf("**Example: %s (%d passages)**\n\n", example_act$act_name[1], nrow(example_act)))

Example: Revenue Act of 1945 (14 passages)

Show code
for (i in 1:min(3, nrow(example_act))) {
  cat(sprintf("**Passage %d:**\n\n", i))
  cat(sprintf("> %s\n\n", str_trunc(example_act$motivation[i], 400)))
}

Passage 1:

full employment in peacetime can be assured only when the reduction in war demand is approximately offset by additional peacetime demand from the millions of consumers, businesses, and farmers

Passage 2:

we must overhaul the wartime tax structure to stimulate consumers’ demand and to promote business investment. The elements of such a tax program should be developed now so that it can be put into effect after victory

Passage 3:

I recommend that a transitional tax bill be enacted as soon as possible to provide limited tax reductions for the calendar year 1946. … [T]he new bill should aim principally at removing barriers to speedy reconversion and to the expansion of our peacetime economy

Key Variables:

act_name: Name of the fiscal act
motivation: Text passage describing the motivation
category: Motivation category (Spending-driven, Countercyclical, Deficit-driven, Long-run)
exogeneity: Exogenous or Endogenous classification
date: Date of the act
source: Source document for the passage



Component 2: Source Data (us_shocks)
Observation Level: Quarter (multiple rows per act, one per quarter of fiscal impact)
Structure: Romer & Romer (2010) dataset with magnitudes and timing of fiscal shocks.


Show code
us_shocks_structure <- us_shocks %>%
  summarize(
    n_quarters = n(),
    n_unique_acts = n_distinct(act_name),
    quarters_per_act_min = min(table(act_name)),
    quarters_per_act_median = median(table(act_name)),
    quarters_per_act_max = max(table(act_name)),
    date_range = paste(min(date_signed, na.rm = TRUE), "to", max(date_signed, na.rm = TRUE))
  )

tibble(
  Metric = c(
    "Total quarters/shocks",
    "Unique acts",
    "Quarters per act (min/median/max)",
    "Date range"
  ),
  Value = c(
    scales::comma(us_shocks_structure$n_quarters),
    as.character(us_shocks_structure$n_unique_acts),
    sprintf("%d / %d / %d",
            us_shocks_structure$quarters_per_act_min,
            us_shocks_structure$quarters_per_act_median,
            us_shocks_structure$quarters_per_act_max),
    us_shocks_structure$date_range
  )
) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(title = "us_shocks: Quarter-Level Structure") %>%
  cols_label(Metric = "Metric", Value = "Value") %>%
  gt_theme_report()

Component 3: Source Data (us_body)
Observation Level: PDF volume (one row per extracted PDF file)
Structure: Extracted text from US government PDF documents (Economic Reports, Budget documents, Treasury Reports). Each row is one PDF file. Most documents correspond to a single PDF, but multi-volume Budget documents (2006-2009) produce multiple rows sharing the same package_id. The package_id is the unique document identifier carried forward into the chunking pipeline.

Component 4: aligned_data (Labels + Shocks)
Transformation: Passage-level to Act-level
Process: Join us_labels with us_shocks to combine human motivations with shock magnitudes/timing.
Key Transformation Logic (from align_labels_shocks() in R/prepare_training_data.R):

Act name cleaning: Normalize whitespace in both datasets
Exact matching: Join on cleaned act names
Fuzzy matching: For unmatched acts, use Jaro-Winkler similarity (threshold = 0.85)
Collapse quarters: Use first quarter’s data as representative (us_shocks has multiple rows per act)
Group passages: Concatenate all passages for each act with \n\n separator

Assumptions:

One representative quarter: When acts have multiple quarters of impact (us_shocks), we use the first quarter’s magnitude and timing as the “primary” shock. This is an acceptable simplification because the LOOCV evaluation tests measure identification, not multi-quarter extraction.
Passage concatenation: All passages describing the same act are concatenated into a single text field. This preserves all human-labeled context while creating a single observation per act.
Fuzzy matching threshold (0.85): Acts with name similarity of at least 0.85 are considered matches. This accommodates minor spelling variations (e.g., “Revenue Act of 1964” vs “Revenue Act, 1964”).

Component 5: chunks (Sliding Window Document Chunks)
Transformation: Document-level to Chunk-level (sliding window)
Process: Split each document in us_body into overlapping chunks that fit within the LLM context window, using make_chunks() in R/make_chunks.R.
Key Logic:

Sliding window: 10-page window with 3-page overlap between consecutive chunks
Token limit: 40,000 tokens (advisory; warns but does not split)
Page separators: Pages joined with --- PAGE BREAK --- markers for page number tracking
Token estimation: Characters / 4 as approximate token count

Design Rationale:
Chunks are the unit of input for C1 evaluation and production. Chunk-based evaluation tests the model under production conditions: long documents containing fiscal measures amid economic commentary, forecasts, and administrative content. This matches the H&K “eval-must-match-production” principle.
Context window budget:
With 10-page windows, chunks are well within the 40K token advisory limit. With prompt overhead (~9K tokens for codebook, few-shot examples, and system instructions) and a 2K output buffer, all chunks fit comfortably within the 200K context window used by both Haiku 3.5 (S1/S2/S3 evaluation) and Sonnet/Opus (production).

Component 6: c1_chunk_data (Positive and Negative Chunks for C1 Evaluation)
Transformation: Identify which chunks are positive (contain a known fiscal measure) and which are negative (contain no fiscal policy discussion), for use in C1 LOOCV evaluation.
Process: prepare_c1_chunk_data() in R/identify_chunk_tiers.R classifies each chunk based on its relationship to the 44 labeled acts in aligned_data.
Positive identification (two tiers):
We identify positive chunks at two levels of stringency:

Tier 1 (verbatim passage): Search for the first 100 characters of each labeled passage (from aligned_data$passages_text) as a substring within year-adjacent chunks. Fallback to 60 characters if no match. These are gold-standard positives: we know the exact Romer and Romer (2010) passage text appears in the chunk.
Tier 2 (act name mention): Search remaining chunks for act name matches using three strategies: whitespace-normalized substring matching (handles OCR line breaks), subcomponent decomposition for compound act names (split on “and”, parenthetical extraction, embedded formal names, Public Law numbers), and co-occurrence matching for event-type names (e.g., “expiration” AND “excess profits”). All strategies require at least 2 fiscal keyword co-occurrences to filter incidental mentions.

Tier 2 adopts a deliberately lax inclusion criterion. Relying on Tier 1 alone would limit positive chunks to those containing verbatim passages from Romer and Romer (2010), which are short excerpts that the original authors selected to illustrate each act’s motivation. Many chunks that substantively discuss an act’s magnitude, timing, or implementation would be missed. By including Tier 2, we ensure the model sees the full range of fiscal discussion surrounding each act, which is essential for downstream codebooks (C2 motivation, C3 timing, C4 magnitude) that require richer context than the labeled passages alone provide.
Negative identification:

Negative: All chunks not assigned to Tier 1 or Tier 2. Each negative is tagged with a continuous key_density score (fiscal keyword count / total words) measuring fiscal vocabulary concentration. This replaces the previous binary relevance-key exclusion with a gradient suitable for stratified error analysis in S3.

Evaluation targets:

Tier 1 Recall (target: 95%) measures whether the model can find known fiscal measures embedded in long documents. Failures here indicate a fundamental problem.
Tier 2 Recall measures generalization beyond verbatim labeled text. Lower recall is expected and informative.
Combined Recall (target: 90%) is the primary gating metric for the C1 funnel.
Negative chunks test precision: can the model correctly reject chunks that do not describe a specific fiscal measure? Negatives span a gradient from clean (zero fiscal vocabulary) to fiscally rich (high key density), and key_density enables stratified precision analysis in S3 to identify the threshold where the model’s discrimination breaks down.

Verification: Are Negatives Correctly Excluded from Positives?
Negatives now intentionally include chunks with fiscal vocabulary (measured by key_density). The verification question is not “are negatives clean?” but “are negatives correctly excluded from positives?” We check whether any negative chunks contain a known act name that should have been caught by Tier 2. A small number is acceptable: act names appearing outside the ±5 year window or without 2+ fiscal keywords are correctly excluded from Tier 2.


Show code
# Check a sample of negative chunks for act name contamination
act_names_pattern <- paste0(
  "(",
  paste(tolower(aligned_data$act_name), collapse = "|"),
  ")"
)

neg_sample <- c1_chunk_data$negatives %>%
  slice_sample(n = min(100, nrow(c1_chunk_data$negatives)))

neg_contamination <- neg_sample %>%
  mutate(
    has_act_name = str_detect(tolower(text), act_names_pattern),
    has_enacted = str_detect(text, regex("\\b(enacted|signed\\s+into\\s+law)\\b",
                                          ignore_case = TRUE))
  ) %>%
  summarize(
    n_checked = n(),
    n_with_act_name = sum(has_act_name),
    n_with_enacted = sum(has_enacted)
  )

tibble(
  Check = c(
    "Negative chunks sampled",
    "Contains known act name",
    "Contains 'enacted/signed into law'"
  ),
  Result = c(
    as.character(neg_contamination$n_checked),
    sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", neg_contamination$n_with_act_name,
            100 * neg_contamination$n_with_act_name / neg_contamination$n_checked),
    sprintf("%d (%.1f%%)", neg_contamination$n_with_enacted,
            100 * neg_contamination$n_with_enacted / neg_contamination$n_checked)
  ),
  Status = c(
    "\u2014",
    ifelse(neg_contamination$n_with_act_name == 0, "Pass", "Review"),
    ifelse(neg_contamination$n_with_enacted == 0, "Pass", "Review")
  )
) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title = "Negative Chunk Contamination Check",
    subtitle = "Verify no act names were missed by Tier 2 matching"
  ) %>%
  cols_label(Check = "Check", Result = "Result", Status = "Status") %>%
  gt_theme_report()

Negatives are all chunks not assigned to Tier 1 or Tier 2. Each negative is tagged with key_density (fiscal keyword concentration) for downstream stratification. Some negatives contain fiscal vocabulary by design; the key question is whether any contain a known act name that Tier 2 should have caught. Act name mentions in negatives are expected when they fall outside the ±5 year window or lack the required 2+ fiscal keyword co-occurrences.
