title: Training Data Overview
text:
Critical Findings

Finding 1: Dataset Size
44 labeled acts are available in us_labels, not 126 from the full us_shocks dataset. This is the number of acts with human-labeled motivation passages, which is the ground truth for all codebook evaluation.
Impact on C1-C4:

C1 (Measure ID): LOOCV on 44 acts with chunk-based inputs. Targets: Combined Recall at least 90%, Tier 1 Recall at least 95%, Precision at least 70%.
C2 (Motivation): With only 6 Countercyclical acts, this category will have limited evaluation power. LOOCV helps by using all 44 acts.
C3/C4 (Timing/Magnitude): 41 of 44 acts have complete timing and magnitude data.
Few-shot budget: Each LOOCV fold uses 43 acts for training. With 5 examples per class, this consumes only ~12% of available acts.



Finding 2: Passage Concatenation Creates Richer Context

Median 8 passages per act (range 1-25), yielding roughly 5K tokens per act
More context improves motivation classification (C2) and information extraction (C3/C4)
Claude’s 200K context window easily handles the longest acts
Higher API costs per call, but LOOCV keeps total calls manageable (44 folds)



Finding 3: Multi-Quarter Acts

us_shocks shows median 7 quarters per act, max 32 quarters
aligned_data uses only the first quarter as representative
C3 (Timing) and C4 (Magnitude) will need to handle multi-quarter extraction in their codebook design
This is a known simplification; the C3/C4 output schemas should support multiple implementation phases



Finding 4: Chunk Tier Coverage


Show code
# Acts with and without Tier 1 coverage
tier1_acts <- n_distinct(c1_chunk_data$tier1$act_name)
tier2_acts <- n_distinct(c1_chunk_data$tier2$act_name)
total_acts <- nrow(aligned_data)

tibble(
  Metric = c(
    "Acts with Tier 1 (verbatim) coverage",
    "Acts with Tier 2 (name mention) coverage",
    "Acts with any chunk coverage",
    "Total labeled acts"
  ),
  Value = c(
    sprintf("%d / %d (%.0f%%)", tier1_acts, total_acts, 100 * tier1_acts / total_acts),
    sprintf("%d / %d (%.0f%%)", tier2_acts, total_acts, 100 * tier2_acts / total_acts),
    sprintf("%d / %d (%.0f%%)",
            n_distinct(c(c1_chunk_data$tier1$act_name, c1_chunk_data$tier2$act_name)),
            total_acts,
            100 * n_distinct(c(c1_chunk_data$tier1$act_name, c1_chunk_data$tier2$act_name)) / total_acts),
    as.character(total_acts)
  )
) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title = "C1 Chunk Tier Coverage",
    subtitle = "How many acts have chunks assigned to each tier"
  ) %>%
  cols_label(Metric = "Metric", Value = "Value") %>%
  gt_theme_report()








C1 Chunk Tier Coverage


How many acts have chunks assigned to each tier


Metric
Value




Acts with Tier 1 (verbatim) coverage
30 / 39 (77%)


Acts with Tier 2 (name mention) coverage
39 / 39 (100%)


Acts with any chunk coverage
39 / 39 (100%)


Total labeled acts
39







Tier 1 coverage indicates how many labeled acts have their verbatim passage text recoverable in the chunked corpus. Acts without Tier 1 coverage may have passages that span chunk boundaries or come from documents not yet extracted. Tier 2 provides additional coverage through whitespace-normalized substring matching, subcomponent decomposition for compound act names, and co-occurrence matching for event-type names. See notebooks/verify_chunk_tiers.qmd for detailed per-act analysis of matching mechanisms and temporal consistency.

title: Training Data Overview
text:
Conclusion
The training data is ready for Phase 0 codebook evaluation with the C1-C4 framework.
Strengths:

100% alignment rate between labels and shocks
Chunk-based evaluation matches production conditions (long documents, fiscal measures amid noise)
Three-tier system provides diagnostic granularity (Tier 1 gold standard, Tier 2 generalization, Negatives for precision)
Tier 2 matching uses whitespace normalization, subcomponent decomposition, and co-occurrence matching to achieve near-complete act coverage
Negative chunks tagged with continuous key_density for stratified precision analysis in S3 (no arbitrary relevance-key exclusion)
Rich passage context (median 8 passages per act) preserved in aligned_data for few-shot examples
LOOCV design uses all 44 acts for both training and evaluation

Known Limitations:

44 acts total: Small sample, but LOOCV maximizes use of available data
Countercyclical under-representation: Only 6 acts in this motivation category
First-quarter simplification: Multi-quarter acts reduced to single representative
Tier 1 coverage gaps: Some acts may lack verbatim passage matches if passages span chunk boundaries or come from unextracted documents
No ground truth for Malaysia: Phase 2 requires expert validation (target: at least 80% agreement on C1)

Evaluation Strategy: Each codebook (C1-C4) is evaluated through the Halterman & Keith 5-stage framework: S0 (codebook prep), S1 (behavioral tests), S2 (LOOCV on 44 acts with bootstrap CIs), S3 (error analysis and ablation), with S4 (fine-tuning) reserved as a last resort. C1 uses chunk-level inputs for S1-S3, with passage-level few-shot examples and chunk-level test inputs.

title: Codebook Development History
text:
S1: Behavioral Tests
S1 results for the latest formal (Haiku/Sonnet) run per codebook, following H&K Figure 3.


Show code
# Latest formal S1 per codebook
latest_s1 <- il$s1 |>
  filter(grepl("^claude-", model)) |>
  group_by(codebook) |>
  filter(iteration == max(iteration)) |>
  ungroup()

plot_s1_behavioral(latest_s1)








Figure 1: S1 behavioral test results. For Tests I–III, higher is better. For Test IV, Fleiss’ Kappa shown.







Show code
latest_s1 |>
  mutate(
    test_label = case_when(
      grepl("^I_", test) ~ "I: Legal Labels",
      grepl("^II_", test) ~ "II: Definition Recovery",
      grepl("^III_", test) ~ "III: Example Recovery",
      grepl("^IV_", test) ~ "IV: Order Invariance"
    ),
    value_fmt = case_when(
      skipped ~ "skipped",
      grepl("IV", test) ~ sprintf("%.1f%% (k=%.2f)", value * 100,
                                   coalesce(fleiss_kappa, NA_real_)),
      TRUE ~ sprintf("%.0f%%", value * 100)
    ),
    threshold_fmt = case_when(
      grepl("IV", test) ~ "< 5%",
      TRUE ~ sprintf(">= %.0f%%", threshold * 100)
    ),
    status = if_else(pass, "Pass", "Fail")
  ) |>
  select(codebook, test_label, value_fmt, threshold_fmt, status) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(
    codebook = "CB", test_label = "Test",
    value_fmt = "Result", threshold_fmt = "Threshold", status = "Status"
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_fill(color = "#E8F5E9"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = status == "Pass")
  ) |>
  gt_theme_report()

title: Training Data Audit
text:
Show code
library(targets)

title: Training Data Audit
text:
Overview
This notebook audits the two ground-truth datasets that anchor Phase 0 codebook development. Both files were re-entered from the Romer & Romer (2010) companion paper using an automated parser (python/parse_rr_companion.py) and verified against the source text for 12 spot-checked acts with zero discrepancies.
The companion paper documents 49 act headings covering the postwar era (1945–2003). Our us_shocks.csv uses the standard series (excluding retroactive changes), which is the baseline R&R recommend for estimating the macroeconomic effects of tax changes.

title: Training Data Audit
text:
1. Act Counts


Show code
shocks_acts <- us_shocks |> distinct(act_name) |> nrow()
labels_acts <- us_labels |> distinct(act_name) |> nrow()

tibble(
  Dataset = c("us_shocks", "us_labels"),
  `Unique Acts` = c(shocks_acts, labels_acts),
  `Total Rows` = c(nrow(us_shocks), nrow(us_labels))
) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Dataset Dimensions") |>
  gt_theme_report()








Dataset Dimensions

Total Rows




us_shocks

The shock data contains 49 acts across 90 rows because many acts have multiple quarterly entries (phased-in tax changes, different provisions taking effect at different times). The labels dataset contains 372 evidence passages across 40 acts. Nine acts lack extracted evidence passages; these are mostly Social Security amendments and highway acts whose companion paper narratives contain few direct quotations from policymakers.

title: Training Data Audit
text:
3. Motivation Category Distribution
R&R classify each tax change along two dimensions: exogeneity (Exogenous vs. Endogenous) and motivation (Spending-driven, Countercyclical, Deficit-driven, Long-run). Only exogenous actions (deficit-driven and long-run) enter the instrumental variable for estimating fiscal multipliers. The distribution across acts determines the effective sample size for codebook evaluation.


Show code
# At the act level (using the dominant motivation)
act_motivations <- us_shocks |>
  group_by(act_name) |>
  summarise(
    primary_category = names(which.max(table(change_in_liabilities_category))),
    primary_exo = names(which.max(table(change_in_liabilities_exo))),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

act_motivations |>
  count(primary_category, primary_exo, name = "n_acts") |>
  arrange(primary_exo, primary_category) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Motivation Distribution Across Acts") |>
  cols_label(
    primary_category = "Motivation",
    primary_exo = "Exogeneity",
    n_acts = "Number of Acts"
  ) |>
  gt_theme_report()








Motivation Distribution Across Acts


Motivation
Exogeneity
Number of Acts




Countercyclical
Endogenous
6


Deficit-driven
Endogenous
5


Spending-driven
Endogenous
12


Deficit-driven
Exogenous
10


Long-run
Exogenous
16







The split is 26 exogenous vs. 23 endogenous acts. Among exogenous acts, long-run motivations dominate (16 acts), followed by deficit-driven (10 acts). No exogenous acts are spending-driven or countercyclical, which is consistent with R&R’s definitions: those motivations inherently respond to the business cycle.


Show code
act_motivations |>
  count(primary_category, primary_exo) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(primary_category, n), y = n, fill = primary_exo)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Acts by Motivation Category",
    x = "Motivation Category",
    y = "Number of Acts",
    fill = "Exogeneity"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Endogenous" = "#E74C3C", "Exogenous" = "#2ECC71"))

title: Training Data Audit
text:
4. Row-Level Motivation Breakdown
Some acts contain rows with different motivation categories. This happens when a single piece of legislation has provisions with distinct economic rationales: for example, a Social Security amendment may pair an immediate spending-driven tax increase with a phased deficit-driven increase years later, or EGTRRA 2001 combines a countercyclical rebate with long-run rate reductions.


Show code
mixed_acts <- us_shocks |>
  group_by(act_name) |>
  summarise(
    n_categories = n_distinct(change_in_liabilities_category),
    categories = paste(unique(change_in_liabilities_category), collapse = " + "),
    n_exo_types = n_distinct(change_in_liabilities_exo),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(n_categories > 1 | n_exo_types > 1)

mixed_acts |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Acts with Mixed Motivations") |>
  cols_label(
    act_name = "Act Name",
    n_categories = "Distinct Categories",
    categories = "Categories",
    n_exo_types = "Distinct Exogeneity"
  ) |>
  gt_theme_report()

2







9 of 49 acts (18%) have mixed motivations. All 9 also have mixed exogeneity, meaning they contain both exogenous and endogenous components. These acts are the hardest cases for codebook C2 (motivation classification) because an LLM must distinguish which provisions fall under which motivation. They will serve as important test cases during S1 behavioral testing and S2 evaluation.

  cat("**Acts in us_labels but NOT in us_shocks:**\n\n")
  cat(paste("-", labels_only, collapse = "\n"))
  cat("\n")

40 of 49 acts appear in both datasets. The 9 acts with shock data but no extracted labels are mostly Social Security amendments and highway acts. Their companion paper narratives are shorter and contain fewer direct quotations, so the quote-extraction regex found no passages meeting the minimum length threshold. This is not a data error: the shock data (quarters, amounts, categories) is complete for all 49 acts regardless of whether quotation evidence was extracted. For codebook C2 evaluation, the full narrative text (stored in the Reasoning column of us_shocks) provides the classification evidence even for acts without separately extracted quotes.

title: Training Data Audit
Training Data Audit
text:
Summary and Assessment


Show code
tibble(
  Metric = c(
    "Unique acts in us_shocks",
    "Unique acts in us_labels",
    "Acts in both datasets",
    "R&R companion paper act headings",
    "Coverage",
    "Exogenous acts (primary)",
    "Endogenous acts (primary)",
    "Mixed-motivation acts",
    "Total evidence passages",
    "Data quality issues"
  ),
  Value = c(
    as.character(shocks_acts),
    as.character(labels_acts),
    as.character(length(in_both)),
    "49",
    sprintf("%.0f%%", shocks_acts / 49 * 100),
    as.character(sum(act_motivations$primary_exo == "Exogenous")),
    as.character(sum(act_motivations$primary_exo == "Endogenous")),
    as.character(nrow(mixed_acts)),
    as.character(nrow(us_labels)),
    "0"
  )
) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = "Training Data Audit Summary") |>
  gt_theme_report()








Training Data Audit Summary


Metric
Value




Unique acts in us_shocks

Coverage
100%

Data quality issues
0







The data is ready for codebook development. All 49 R&R act headings are present with complete shock data (quarters, amounts, categories, present values). The key properties that support Phase 0 evaluation are:

Full R&R coverage. 100% of companion paper act headings are captured, providing the complete benchmark against which codebooks C1–C4 will be evaluated.
Clean, consistent categories. Four motivation types and two exogeneity types are consistently applied across all 90 rows with no missing values.
Balanced exogeneity split. The 26/23 exogenous/endogenous split provides adequate representation of both classes for LOOCV evaluation. C2 codebook precision targets (Exogenous Precision ≥85%) are evaluable against 26 exogenous acts.
Mixed-motivation complexity. 9 acts (18%) have mixed motivations, providing hard test cases for C2 behavioral testing (S1) and error analysis (S3).
Rich evidence base. 372 extracted quotations across 40 acts supply the few-shot examples and ground-truth reasoning for codebook development.







Note



The 9 acts without extracted label passages still have complete narrative text in the Reasoning column of us_shocks. Codebook C2 evaluation uses the full act narrative, not the separately extracted quotes. The us_labels dataset primarily supports few-shot example construction and motivation-evidence traceability.

title: Pre-Flight API Input Verification
text:
Before running expensive S2 LOOCV API calls, this notebook runs 12 systematic checks on the data pipeline outputs. Each test produces a PASS/WARN/FAIL result. All tests must pass (or have documented exceptions) before proceeding.


Show code
pacman::p_load(targets, tidyverse, gt, here, jsonlite)

here::i_am("notebooks/verify_api_inputs.qmd")
tar_config_set(store = here("_targets"))
source(here("R/gt_theme.R"))
source(here("R/identify_chunk_tiers.R"))
source(here("R/codebook_stage_0.R"))
source(here("R/generate_c1_examples.R"))
set_theme(theme_minimal())




Show code
us_body      <- tar_read(us_body)
chunks       <- tar_read(chunks)
aligned_data <- tar_read(aligned_data)
c1_chunk_data <- tar_read(c1_chunk_data)
c1_codebook  <- tar_read(c1_codebook)




Show code
# Accumulator for test results
test_results <- tibble(
  test_id = integer(),
  test_name = character(),
  result = character(),
  status = character()
)

add_result <- function(id, name, result, status) {
  test_results <<- bind_rows(
    test_results,
    tibble(test_id = id, test_name = name, result = result, status = status)
  )
}

Pre-Flight API Input Verification
text:
Test 4: Act Name Alignment
The act names in aligned_data should match exactly with the act names in the tier data (both directions).


Show code
aligned_acts <- sort(unique(aligned_data$act_name))
tier_acts <- sort(unique(c(
  c1_chunk_data$tier1$act_name,
  c1_chunk_data$tier2$act_name
)))

in_aligned_not_tiers <- setdiff(aligned_acts, tier_acts)
in_tiers_not_aligned <- setdiff(tier_acts, aligned_acts)

alignment_ok <- length(in_aligned_not_tiers) == 0 && length(in_tiers_not_aligned) == 0

add_result(
  4, "Act name alignment",
  sprintf("Aligned→Tiers: %d missing, Tiers→Aligned: %d extra",
          length(in_aligned_not_tiers), length(in_tiers_not_aligned)),
  if (alignment_ok) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)



Show code
if (length(in_aligned_not_tiers) > 0) {
  cat("\n\n**Acts in aligned_data but not in tiers:**\n\n")
  for (act in in_aligned_not_tiers) cat(sprintf("- %s\n", act))
}
if (length(in_tiers_not_aligned) > 0) {
  cat("\n\n**Acts in tiers but not in aligned_data:**\n\n")
  for (act in in_tiers_not_aligned) cat(sprintf("- %s\n", act))
}

Test 4: PASS

title: Pre-Flight API Input Verification
Pre-Flight API Input Verification
Pre-Flight API Input Verification
text:
Test 7: No Within-Tier Duplicates
Tier 1 should have no duplicate (doc_id, chunk_id, act_name, passage_idx) combinations. Tier 2 should have no duplicate (doc_id, chunk_id, act_name) combinations. Multiple passages matching the same chunk are expected for Tier 1 and flagged as WARN.


Show code
# Tier 1: true duplicates (same chunk, act, passage)
tier1_n_before <- nrow(c1_chunk_data$tier1)
tier1_n_distinct <- c1_chunk_data$tier1 |>
  distinct(doc_id, chunk_id, act_name, passage_idx) |>
  nrow()
tier1_dupes <- tier1_n_before - tier1_n_distinct

# Tier 1: multi-passage matches (same chunk, different passages)
tier1_multi <- c1_chunk_data$tier1 |>
  count(doc_id, chunk_id, act_name) |>
  filter(n > 1) |>
  nrow()

# Tier 2: true duplicates
tier2_n_before <- nrow(c1_chunk_data$tier2)
tier2_n_distinct <- c1_chunk_data$tier2 |>
  distinct(doc_id, chunk_id, act_name) |>
  nrow()
tier2_dupes <- tier2_n_before - tier2_n_distinct

total_dupes <- tier1_dupes + tier2_dupes

add_result(
  7, "No within-tier duplicates",
  sprintf("T1: %d true dupes, %d multi-passage chunks; T2: %d dupes",
          tier1_dupes, tier1_multi, tier2_dupes),
  if (total_dupes == 0 && tier1_multi == 0) "PASS"
  else if (total_dupes == 0 && tier1_multi > 0) "WARN"
  else "FAIL"
)


Test 7: PASS

title: Pre-Flight API Input Verification
text:
Test 8: Token Budget Fit
All chunks plus overhead (codebook prompt + few-shot examples + system prompt + output buffer) must fit within the 200K context window.


Show code
# Compute actual codebook prompt tokens
codebook_prompt <- construct_codebook_prompt(c1_codebook)
codebook_tokens <- nchar(codebook_prompt) / 4

# Overhead components
few_shot_tokens <- 3100   # estimated from generate_c1_loocv_fold_examples()
system_tokens <- 125      # system prompt
output_tokens <- 2000     # output buffer
overhead <- codebook_tokens + few_shot_tokens + system_tokens + output_tokens

context_limit <- 200000

# Get max tokens across all tiers
max_tier1_tokens <- if (nrow(c1_chunk_data$tier1) > 0) max(c1_chunk_data$tier1$approx_tokens, na.rm = TRUE) else 0
max_tier2_tokens <- if (nrow(c1_chunk_data$tier2) > 0) max(c1_chunk_data$tier2$approx_tokens, na.rm = TRUE) else 0
max_neg_tokens <- if (nrow(c1_chunk_data$negatives) > 0) max(c1_chunk_data$negatives$approx_tokens, na.rm = TRUE) else 0
max_chunk_tokens <- max(max_tier1_tokens, max_tier2_tokens, max_neg_tokens)

total_max <- max_chunk_tokens + overhead
fits <- total_max < context_limit

budget_tbl <- tibble(
  component = c("Codebook prompt", "Few-shot examples", "System prompt",
                "Output buffer", "Max chunk", "Total"),
  tokens = c(round(codebook_tokens), few_shot_tokens, system_tokens,
             output_tokens, max_chunk_tokens, round(total_max))
)

budget_tbl |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(component = "Component", tokens = "Tokens (est.)") |>
  fmt_number(columns = tokens, decimals = 0) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_fill(color = if (fits) "#ccffcc" else "#ffcccc"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = component == "Total")
  ) |>
  gt_theme_report()

Show code
add_result(
  8, "Token budget fit",
  sprintf("Max total: %s / %s", scales::comma(round(total_max)), scales::comma(context_limit)),
  if (fits) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)


Test 8: PASS (max 67,905 tokens vs. 200,000 limit)

title: Pre-Flight API Input Verification
text:
Test 9: Text Encoding Safety
Sample chunks from each tier and check for null bytes and control characters that could corrupt API calls.


Show code
# Control character pattern (excludes \x00 which R can't store, and \t \n \r which are safe)
control_pattern <- "[\x01-\x08\x0b\x0c\x0e-\x1f]"

check_encoding <- function(texts, label) {
  n <- min(100, length(texts))
  if (n == 0) return(tibble(tier = label, n_checked = 0, n_unsafe = 0))
  sample_texts <- texts[sample(length(texts), n)]
  n_unsafe <- sum(str_detect(sample_texts, control_pattern))
  tibble(tier = label, n_checked = n, n_unsafe = n_unsafe)
}

set.seed(44)
encoding_results <- bind_rows(
  check_encoding(c1_chunk_data$tier1$text, "Tier 1"),
  check_encoding(c1_chunk_data$tier2$text, "Tier 2"),
  check_encoding(c1_chunk_data$negatives$text, "Negative")
)

total_unsafe <- sum(encoding_results$n_unsafe)

encoding_results |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(tier = "Tier", n_checked = "Sampled", n_unsafe = "Unsafe") |>
  gt_theme_report()








Tier
Sampled
Unsafe




Tier 1
70
0


Tier 2
100
0


Negative
100
0







Show code
add_result(
  9, "Text encoding safety",
  sprintf("%d unsafe chars in %d sampled chunks",
          total_unsafe, sum(encoding_results$n_checked)),
  if (total_unsafe == 0) "PASS" else "WARN"
)


Test 9: PASS

title: Pre-Flight API Input Verification
text:
Test 10: Year Filter Consistency
The tier data should respect the max_doc_year = 2007 parameter. No chunk in Tier 1 or Tier 2 should come from a document published after 2007.


Show code
max_doc_year <- 2007L

tier1_max_year <- if (nrow(c1_chunk_data$tier1) > 0) max(c1_chunk_data$tier1$year, na.rm = TRUE) else NA
tier2_max_year <- if (nrow(c1_chunk_data$tier2) > 0) max(c1_chunk_data$tier2$year, na.rm = TRUE) else NA

year_ok <- (is.na(tier1_max_year) || tier1_max_year <= max_doc_year) &&
           (is.na(tier2_max_year) || tier2_max_year <= max_doc_year)

add_result(
  10, "Year filter consistency",
  sprintf("Tier 1 max year: %s, Tier 2 max year: %s (limit: %d)",
          as.character(tier1_max_year), as.character(tier2_max_year), max_doc_year),
  if (year_ok) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)


Test 10: PASS

title: Pre-Flight API Input Verification
text:
Test 11: Few-Shot Example Preview
Generate examples for one LOOCV fold (first act held out) and verify they render correctly. This confirms that generate_c1_loocv_fold_examples() produces usable training data.


Show code
# Hold out the first act
held_out_act <- aligned_data$act_name[1]
train_data <- aligned_data |> filter(act_name != held_out_act)

examples <- generate_c1_loocv_fold_examples(
  train_data,
  c1_chunk_data$negatives,
  n_per_class = 5,
  codebook = c1_codebook,
  seed = 20251206
)

n_examples <- length(examples)
n_positive <- sum(map_chr(examples, ~ .x$output$label) == "FISCAL_MEASURE")
n_negative <- n_examples - n_positive

add_result(
  11, "Few-shot example preview",
  sprintf("%d examples (%d pos, %d neg) for fold '%s'",
          n_examples, n_positive, n_negative,
          str_trunc(held_out_act, 30)),
  if (n_examples >= 2) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)



Show code
cat(sprintf("\n\n**Held-out act:** %s\n\n", held_out_act))

Held-out act: Changes in Depreciation Guidelines and Revenue Act of 1962

Show code
cat(sprintf("**Examples generated:** %d (%d positive, %d negative)\n\n",
            n_examples, n_positive, n_negative))

Examples generated: 10 (5 positive, 5 negative)

Show code
for (i in seq_along(examples)) {
  ex <- examples[[i]]
  label <- ex$output$label
  act_name <- ex$output$measure_name
  reasoning <- ex$output$reasoning
  input_trunc <- str_trunc(ex$input, 400)

  cat(sprintf("\n\n#### Example %d: %s\n\n", i, label))
  if (!is.null(act_name)) cat(sprintf("**Act:** %s\n\n", act_name))
  cat(sprintf("**Reasoning:** %s\n\n", reasoning))
  cat(sprintf("**Input (truncated):**\n\n> %s\n\n",
              str_replace_all(input_trunc, "\n", "\n> ")))
}

title: C2 Input Verification
text:
Before building the C2 codebook or running evaluation, this notebook verifies the C1→C2 data handoff. Two targets are checked:

c1_classified_chunks: C1 S2 classifications merged with chunk text and ground truth labels from aligned_data
c2_input_data: Filtered to pred_label == "FISCAL_MEASURE" and discusses_motivation == TRUE

Eight tests check coverage, label completeness, text joins, C1 output fields, filter correctness, post-filter act coverage, class balance, and token distribution.


Show code
pacman::p_load(targets, tidyverse, gt, here, scales)

here::i_am("notebooks/verify_c2_inputs.qmd")
tar_config_set(store = here("_targets"))
source(here("R/gt_theme.R"))




Show code
c1_classified <- tar_read(c1_classified_chunks)
c2_input      <- tar_read(c2_input_data)
aligned_data  <- tar_read(aligned_data)




Show code
test_results <- tibble(
  test_id = integer(),
  test_name = character(),
  result = character(),
  status = character()
)

add_result <- function(id, name, result, status) {
  test_results <<- bind_rows(
    test_results,
    tibble(test_id = id, test_name = name, result = result, status = status)
  )
}

title: C2 Input Verification
text:
Part A: c1_classified_chunks (Precursor)

Test 1: Act Coverage
Every act in aligned_data should appear in c1_classified_chunks.


Show code
aligned_acts <- sort(unique(aligned_data$act_name))
classified_acts <- sort(unique(c1_classified$act_name))

missing <- setdiff(aligned_acts, classified_acts)
extra <- setdiff(classified_acts, aligned_acts)
coverage_ok <- length(missing) == 0 && length(extra) == 0

add_result(
  1, "Act coverage",
  sprintf("%d/%d acts present", length(classified_acts), length(aligned_acts)),
  if (coverage_ok) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)



Show code
if (length(missing) > 0) {
  cat("\n\n**Acts in aligned_data but missing from c1_classified_chunks:**\n\n")
  for (act in missing) cat(sprintf("- %s\n", act))
}
if (length(extra) > 0) {
  cat("\n\n**Acts in c1_classified_chunks but not in aligned_data:**\n\n")
  for (act in extra) cat(sprintf("- %s\n", act))
}

Test 1: PASS


Test 2: Label Completeness
Every row must have non-NA motivation_category and exogenous_flag.


Show code
n_missing_motivation <- sum(is.na(c1_classified$motivation_category))
n_missing_exogenous <- sum(is.na(c1_classified$exogenous_flag))
labels_ok <- n_missing_motivation == 0 && n_missing_exogenous == 0

add_result(
  2, "Label completeness",
  sprintf("motivation: %d NA, exogenous: %d NA",
          n_missing_motivation, n_missing_exogenous),
  if (labels_ok) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)


Test 2: PASS


Test 3: Text Join Completeness
All rows should have non-NA, non-empty text after joining from chunks.


Show code
n_na_text <- sum(is.na(c1_classified$text))
n_empty_text <- sum(!is.na(c1_classified$text) & nchar(c1_classified$text) == 0)
text_ok <- n_na_text == 0 && n_empty_text == 0

add_result(
  3, "Text join completeness",
  sprintf("%d NA, %d empty (of %d rows)", n_na_text, n_empty_text,
          nrow(c1_classified)),
  if (text_ok) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)


Test 3: PASS


Test 4: C1 Output Field Completeness
pred_label should be non-NA for all rows. Report the distribution of discusses_motivation, discusses_timing, and discusses_magnitude.


Show code
n_na_pred <- sum(is.na(c1_classified$pred_label))
pred_ok <- n_na_pred == 0

add_result(

          n_na_pred,

          sum(c1_classified$discusses_motivation == FALSE, na.rm = TRUE),
          sum(is.na(c1_classified$discusses_motivation))),
  if (pred_ok) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)

title: C2 Input Verification
text:
Part B: c2_input_data (Filtered)

Test 5: Filter Correctness
All rows must have pred_label == "FISCAL_MEASURE" and discusses_motivation == TRUE.


Show code
n_wrong_label <- sum(c2_input$pred_label != "FISCAL_MEASURE", na.rm = TRUE)
n_wrong_motiv <- sum(c2_input$discusses_motivation != TRUE, na.rm = TRUE)
filter_ok <- n_wrong_label == 0 && n_wrong_motiv == 0

add_result(
  5, "Filter correctness",
  sprintf("Non-FISCAL_MEASURE: %d, discusses_motivation != TRUE: %d",
          n_wrong_label, n_wrong_motiv),
  if (filter_ok) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)


Test 5: PASS


Test 6: Act Coverage Post-Filter
How many aligned acts survive the motivation filter? Acts that lose all chunks cannot be evaluated by C2.


Show code
c2_acts <- unique(c2_input$act_name)
lost_acts <- setdiff(aligned_acts, c2_acts)
n_lost <- length(lost_acts)
post_filter_ok <- n_lost == 0

add_result(
  6, "Act coverage post-filter",
  sprintf("%d/%d acts retained (%d lost)",
          length(c2_acts), length(aligned_acts), n_lost),
  if (post_filter_ok) "PASS" else "WARN"
)



Show code
if (n_lost > 0) {
  cat("\n\n**Acts with no FISCAL_MEASURE + discusses_motivation chunks:**\n\n")
  lost_info <- aligned_data |>
    filter(act_name %in% lost_acts) |>
    select(act_name, year, motivation_category) |>
    distinct()
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(lost_info))) {
    cat(sprintf("- %s (%d) — %s\n",
                lost_info$act_name[i], lost_info$year[i],
                lost_info$motivation_category[i]))
  }
}

Test 6: PASS


Test 7: Motivation Class Balance
Distribution of motivation_category across C2 input chunks, compared to aligned_data.


Show code

balance_check <- c2_act_dist |>

    n_c2 = replace_na(n_c2, 0L),
    n_aligned = replace_na(n_aligned, 0L),
    match = n_c2 == n_aligned
  )

balance_ok <- all(balance_check$match)

add_result(

          sum(balance_check$match), nrow(balance_check)),

Test 8: Token Distribution
Per-chunk token distribution. Chunks were constructed to fit within the context window, so all should be well under 200K.


Show code
token_summary <- c2_input |>
  summarise(
    min = min(approx_tokens),
    p25 = quantile(approx_tokens, 0.25),
    median = median(approx_tokens),
    mean = round(mean(approx_tokens)),

    max = max(approx_tokens)

overhead_tokens <- 10000
context_limit <- 200000
effective_limit <- context_limit - overhead_tokens
n_over <- sum(c2_input$approx_tokens > effective_limit)
tokens_ok <- n_over == 0

add_result(
  8, "Token budget fit",
  sprintf("Max: %s tokens (limit: %s)",
          comma(token_summary$max), comma(effective_limit)),
  if (tokens_ok) "PASS" else "FAIL"
)

title: Deployment Corpus Verification
text:
This notebook verifies the text-extraction quality of every country in the deployment pipeline (currently Malaysia; future Indonesia, Thailand, Philippines, Vietnam). It runs on country_body and country_chunks after they are built, before C1 deployment.
The US precedent is verify_body.qmd. Six of its seven tests port directly: URL resolution, boundary documents, temporal coverage, text quality, anomaly detection, and a new chunk-integrity test that the US notebook does not run. The seventh US test, “Known Act Validation”, is omitted because deployment countries have no labeled passages. Anchor-event detection (1997 Asian Financial Crisis, 2015 GST, 2020 COVID packages) is reserved for the C1-output deployment evaluation notebook, not here.


Show code
pacman::p_load(targets, tidyverse, tinytable, here, digest, stringi)

here::i_am("notebooks/verify_country_body.qmd")
tar_config_set(store = here("_targets"))
source(here("R/tt_theme.R"))
theme_set(theme_minimal())

# Country-branched targets use iteration = "list"; bind each into one tibble.
country_urls    <- tar_read(country_urls)    |> bind_rows()
country_body    <- tar_read(country_body)    |> bind_rows()
country_chunks  <- tar_read(country_chunks)  |> bind_rows()
country_configs <- tar_read(country_configs)

# Flatten country_configs list for joins
configs_tbl <- tibble(
  country          = map_chr(country_configs, "country"),
  pilot_year_min   = map_int(country_configs, "pilot_year_min"),
  pilot_year_max   = map_int(country_configs, "pilot_year_max"),
  primary_language = map_chr(country_configs, "primary_language")
)

# Join chunks back to body metadata so country/source/body are available
chunks_meta <- country_chunks |>
  left_join(
    country_body |> select(package_id, country, source, body, doc_language),
    by = c("doc_id" = "package_id")
  )

# Helper: apply PASS/WARN/FAIL/INFO/SKIP colors to a tinytable status column.
# Takes a tt object, the raw status vector (used to find row indices), and the
# column position (integer) in the rendered table to color.
style_status <- function(tt_obj, status_vec, j) {
  colors <- c(
    PASS = "#2E7D32", WARN = "#F57C00",
    FAIL = "#C62828", INFO = "#1565C0",
    SKIP = "#757575"
  )
  for (s in names(colors)) {
    rows <- which(status_vec == s)
    if (length(rows) > 0) {
      tt_obj <- tinytable::style_tt(
        tt_obj, i = rows, j = j,
        color = colors[[s]], bold = TRUE
      )
    }
  }
  tt_obj
}

# Test-result accumulator: one row per (country, test)
test_results <- tibble(
  country = character(),
  test    = character(),
  metric  = character(),
  value   = character(),
  target  = character(),
  status  = character()
)
record_result <- function(country, test, metric, value, target, status) {
  test_results <<- bind_rows(test_results, tibble(
    country = country, test = test, metric = metric,
    value = as.character(value), target = target, status = status
  ))
}






Show code
chunks_per_country <- chunks_meta |>
  count(country, name = "Chunks")

corpus_snapshot <- country_body |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarize(
    Documents          = n(),
    Extracted          = sum(n_pages > 0),
    Pages              = sum(n_pages),
    `Year range`       = sprintf("%d-%d", min(year), max(year)),
    `Document bodies`  = n_distinct(body),
    Sources            = n_distinct(source),
    `OCR docs`         = sum(ocr_used, na.rm = TRUE),
    `OCR pages`        = sum(n_pages_ocr, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups            = "drop"
  ) |>
  left_join(chunks_per_country, by = "country")

corpus_snapshot |>
  mutate(across(c(Documents, Extracted, Pages, `OCR docs`, `OCR pages`, Chunks),
                ~ scales::comma(.x))) |>
  rename(Country = country) |>
  tt() |>
  tt_theme_report()

                Country
                Documents
                Extracted
                Pages
                Year range
                Document bodies
                Sources
                OCR docs
                OCR pages
                Chunks
              
        
        
        
                
                  malaysia
                  162
                  107
                  20,945
                  1980-2022
                  6
                  10
                  48
                  5,010
                  3,033
                
        
      
    








Show code

  filter(n_pages > 0) |>
  group_by(country, year) |>
  summarize(pages = sum(n_pages), .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = pages)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.85) +
  facet_wrap(~ country, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Extracted pages")

title: Deployment Corpus Verification
section: Test (i): URL resolution and extraction success
text:
Test (i): URL resolution and extraction success
For each country, the share of documents in country_urls that produced extracted text in country_body. Documents with access_status == "manual_pending" are reported separately as known acquisition gaps, not extraction failures.


Show code
# Join URL manifest to body to classify each expected document
url_status <- country_urls |>
  left_join(
    country_body |> select(country, package_id, n_pages),
    by = c("country", "package_id")
  ) |>
  mutate(
    n_pages = replace_na(n_pages, 0L),
    outcome = case_when(
      access_status == "manual_pending"               ~ "Pending",
      access_status %in% c("auto", "manual_ready") &
        n_pages > 0                                   ~ "Extracted",
      access_status %in% c("auto", "manual_ready") &
        n_pages == 0                                  ~ "Failed",
      TRUE                                            ~ "Failed"
    )
  )

test_i_summary <- url_status |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarize(
    n_expected      = n(),
    n_pending       = sum(outcome == "Pending"),
    n_resolvable    = sum(outcome != "Pending"),
    n_extracted     = sum(outcome == "Extracted"),
    n_failed        = sum(outcome == "Failed"),
    success_rate    = if (n_resolvable > 0) n_extracted / n_resolvable else NA_real_,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    status = case_when(
      is.na(success_rate)   ~ "INFO",
      success_rate >= 0.95  ~ "PASS",
      success_rate >= 0.85  ~ "WARN",
      TRUE                  ~ "FAIL"
    )
  )

for (i in seq_len(nrow(test_i_summary))) {
  r <- test_i_summary[i, ]
  record_result(
    r$country, "i", "URL resolution rate (excl. pending)",
    sprintf("%.1f%% (%d/%d)", 100 * r$success_rate, r$n_extracted, r$n_resolvable),
    "≥95%", r$status
  )
}




Show code
test_i_summary |>
  mutate(
    `Success rate` = sprintf("%.1f%%", 100 * success_rate)
  ) |>
  select(
    Country     = country,
    Expected    = n_expected,
    Pending     = n_pending,
    Resolvable  = n_resolvable,
    Extracted   = n_extracted,
    Failed      = n_failed,
    `Success rate`,
    Status      = status
  ) |>
  tt() |>
  style_status(test_i_summary$status, j = 8) |>
  tt_theme_report()

Test (i) is the headline gate. Pending rows reflect the manual-acquisition workflow and are tracked but excluded from the success-rate denominator. A FAIL here points to URL resolution or PDF-extraction problems and must be fixed before C1 deployment runs.

title: Deployment Corpus Verification
text:
Test (ii): Boundary documents
For every country × source × body combination, the earliest and latest documents with extracted text must be at least 10 pages long. This catches systematic extraction failure at the edges of the corpus where document formats are most likely to drift.


Show code
boundary_docs <- bind_rows(
  country_body |>
    filter(n_pages > 0) |>
    group_by(country, source, body) |>
    slice_min(year, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
    mutate(boundary_type = "Earliest") |>
    ungroup(),
  country_body |>
    filter(n_pages > 0) |>
    group_by(country, source, body) |>
    slice_max(year, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
    mutate(boundary_type = "Latest") |>
    ungroup()
)

test_ii_per_country <- boundary_docs |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarize(
    n_boundary = n(),
    n_short    = sum(n_pages < 10),
    status     = if_else(n_short == 0, "PASS", "WARN"),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

for (i in seq_len(nrow(test_ii_per_country))) {
  r <- test_ii_per_country[i, ]
  record_result(
    r$country, "ii", "Boundary docs ≥10 pages",
    sprintf("%d/%d", r$n_boundary - r$n_short, r$n_boundary),
    "All ≥10 pages", r$status
  )
}




Show code
boundary_docs |>
  select(country, source, body, year, boundary_type, n_pages, ocr_used) |>
  arrange(country, source, body, boundary_type) |>
  rename(
    Country   = country, Source = source, Body = body, Year = year,
    Boundary  = boundary_type, Pages = n_pages, OCR = ocr_used

title: Deployment Corpus Verification
Deployment Corpus Verification
Deployment Corpus Verification
Deployment Corpus Verification
text:
Test (vi): Chunk integrity
The US verify_body.qmd stops at body-level checks, but the deployment pipeline feeds C1 from country_chunks. This test verifies the chunk layer.


Show code
CONTEXT_BUDGET_TOKENS <- 190700L     # 200K minus ~9.3K codebook+fewshot+output overhead
MIN_CHARS              <- 100L        # make_chunks() default
PAGE_BREAK_MARKER      <- "--- PAGE BREAK ---"

# Per-document expected end_page comes from country_body
doc_totals <- country_body |>
  filter(n_pages > 0) |>
  select(package_id, country, total_pages = n_pages)

chunk_checks <- chunks_meta |>
  left_join(doc_totals |> select(-country), by = c("doc_id" = "package_id")) |>
  group_by(country, doc_id) |>
  arrange(chunk_id, .by_group = TRUE) |>
  summarize(
    first_chunk_starts_at_1 = first(start_page) == 1,
    last_chunk_ends_at_total = last(end_page) == first(total_pages),
    chunk_ids_contiguous = identical(chunk_id, seq_len(n())),
    any_short_chunk = any(nchar(text) <= MIN_CHARS),
    any_oversized_chunk = any(approx_tokens > CONTEXT_BUDGET_TOKENS),
    marker_count_ok = all(
      str_count(text, fixed(PAGE_BREAK_MARKER)) == pmax(n_pages - 1, 0)
    ),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    doc_passes = first_chunk_starts_at_1 & last_chunk_ends_at_total &
      chunk_ids_contiguous & !any_short_chunk & !any_oversized_chunk &
      marker_count_ok
  )

test_vi_per_country <- chunk_checks |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarize(
    n_docs           = n(),
    n_passing        = sum(doc_passes),
    n_starts_bad     = sum(!first_chunk_starts_at_1),
    n_ends_bad       = sum(!last_chunk_ends_at_total),
    n_gaps           = sum(!chunk_ids_contiguous),
    n_short          = sum(any_short_chunk),
    n_oversized      = sum(any_oversized_chunk),
    n_markers_bad    = sum(!marker_count_ok),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    status = if_else(n_passing == n_docs, "PASS", "FAIL")
  )

for (i in seq_len(nrow(test_vi_per_country))) {
  r <- test_vi_per_country[i, ]
  record_result(
    r$country, "vi", "Chunk-integrity-pass docs",
    sprintf("%d/%d", r$n_passing, r$n_docs),
    "All pass", r$status
  )
}




Show code
test_vi_per_country |>
  select(
    Country         = country,
    Documents       = n_docs,
    Passing         = n_passing,
    `Bad start`     = n_starts_bad,
    `Bad end`       = n_ends_bad,
    `Gap in IDs`    = n_gaps,
    `Short chunks`  = n_short,
    Oversized       = n_oversized,
    `Bad markers`   = n_markers_bad,
    Status          = status
  ) |>
  tt() |>
  style_status(test_vi_per_country$status, j = 10) |>
  tt_theme_report()

Show code
chunks_meta |>
  ggplot(aes(x = approx_tokens)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.75) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = CONTEXT_BUDGET_TOKENS,
             linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  facet_wrap(~ country, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(x = "Approximate tokens", y = "Chunks") +
  annotate("text", x = CONTEXT_BUDGET_TOKENS,
           y = Inf, label = "Available context",
           hjust = 1.05, vjust = 1.5, color = "red", size = 3)








Figure 7: Chunk token distribution with available-context reference line





Any FAIL here is a pipeline bug, not a corpus problem. The most likely failure modes are: a document whose extraction returned zero pages but still produced a chunk row, a marker-count mismatch from a stray --- PAGE BREAK --- inside the source text, or an oversized chunk produced by a very long page that the sliding-window logic could not split.

title: Deployment Corpus Verification
text:
Test (vii): Language audit
doc_language claims English for all current Malaysia rows. Bahasa Malaysia contamination is plausible for Hansard fallbacks and bilingual editions. Per chunk we compare a small Bahasa stopword list against an English stopword list and flag chunks where the Bahasa share exceeds the English share.


Show code
LANGUAGE_SAMPLE_PER_COUNTRY <- 500L

en_stopwords <- c("\\bthe\\b", "\\band\\b", "\\bof\\b", "\\bto\\b",
                  "\\bin\\b", "\\bfor\\b", "\\bis\\b", "\\bwith\\b")
bm_stopwords <- c("\\byang\\b", "\\bdan\\b", "\\buntuk\\b", "\\bialah\\b",
                  "\\bdengan\\b", "\\badalah\\b", "\\bkepada\\b", "\\bpada\\b")

count_matches <- function(text, patterns) {
  rgx <- regex(paste(patterns, collapse = "|"), ignore_case = TRUE)
  str_count(text, rgx)
}

set.seed(43)
lang_sample <- chunks_meta |>
  group_by(country) |>
  group_modify(~ slice_sample(
    .x, n = min(LANGUAGE_SAMPLE_PER_COUNTRY, nrow(.x))
  )) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(
    en_count = count_matches(text, en_stopwords),
    bm_count = count_matches(text, bm_stopwords),
    bm_dominant = bm_count > en_count & bm_count >= 5
  )

test_vii_per_country <- lang_sample |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarize(
    n_sampled       = n(),
    n_bm_dominant   = sum(bm_dominant),
    pct_bm_dominant = mean(bm_dominant),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    # Language audit is diagnostic, never a deployment blocker.
    # Any Bahasa-dominant chunks in claimed-English docs => WARN.
    status = if_else(n_bm_dominant == 0, "PASS", "WARN")
  )

for (i in seq_len(nrow(test_vii_per_country))) {
  r <- test_vii_per_country[i, ]
  record_result(
    r$country, "vii", "Bahasa-dominant chunks (claimed English)",
    sprintf("%d/%d (%.1f%%)", r$n_bm_dominant, r$n_sampled,
            100 * r$pct_bm_dominant),
    "0", r$status
  )
}




Show code
test_vii_per_country |>
  mutate(`Bahasa-dominant rate` = sprintf("%.1f%%", 100 * pct_bm_dominant)) |>
  select(
    Country                   = country,
    `Chunks sampled`          = n_sampled,
    `Bahasa-dominant chunks`  = n_bm_dominant,
    `Bahasa-dominant rate`,
    Status                    = status
  ) |>
  tt() |>
  style_status(test_vii_per_country$status, j = 5) |>
  tt_theme_report()

Show code

  ggplot(aes(x = en_count, y = bm_count, color = bm_dominant)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  facet_wrap(~ country, ncol = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "#C62828", `FALSE` = "#1565C0")) +
  labs(x = "English stopword count", y = "Bahasa stopword count",
       color = "Bahasa-dominant")








Figure 8: Per-chunk English vs Bahasa stopword density





A small number of Bahasa-dominant chunks is plausible (front matter, signage pages, bilingual sections); a large number suggests a series is misclassified at the doc_language field and the codebook prompt’s language assumption may break.

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
The problem. For most emerging markets, we lack consistent and comparable measures of exogenous fiscal shocks: the cornerstone input for credible fiscal multiplier estimation and fiscal policy analysis. The gold-standard “narrative approach” (Romer and Romer 2010) has been applied to the United States, where it required years of manual work. For low- and middle-income countries, replication costs have been prohibitive.
Our contribution. This project builds a validated, LLM-assisted methodology for fiscal shock identification that can transfer across countries without retraining. We integrate the Romer & Romer methodology with a rigorous LLM evaluation framework (Halterman and Keith 2025) to produce the first systematic application of modern AI evaluation standards to economic history research.
Current status. We have built the document infrastructure (approximately 97,000 pages of US fiscal documents extracted and structured) and demonstrated through a proof-of-concept that LLMs can identify fiscal policy language with high accuracy. We are now implementing a four-codebook architecture with systematic validation before deployment.
What we’re asking

API budget for development phase: ~$200–250 (codebook validation through early March)
API budget for production phase: ~$250–400 (US and Malaysia deployment, contingent on development results)
Identification of a Malaysia fiscal policy expert for validation (~25–40 hours)

The development phase produces a clear feasibility signal: if codebooks do not meet performance targets on US ground-truth data by early March, we will know the approach is not viable before committing to production costs.
Timeline

Full codebook evaluation suite validated: Early March 2026 (go/no-go decision point)
US benchmark deployment with ground-truth comparison: Late March 2026
Malaysia pilot deployment: April 2026
Full Southeast Asia dataset (Indonesia, Thailand, Philippines, Vietnam): June 2026
crumbs: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
The problem. For most emerging markets, we lack consistent and comparable measures of exogenous fiscal shocks: the cornerstone input for credible fiscal multiplier estimation and fiscal policy analysis. The gold-standard “narrative approach” (Romer and Romer 2010) has been applied to the United States, where it required years of manual work. For low- and middle-income countries, replication costs have been prohibitive.
Our contribution. This project builds a validated, LLM-assisted methodology for fiscal shock identification that can transfer across countries without retraining. We integrate the Romer & Romer methodology with a rigorous LLM evaluation framework (Halterman and Keith 2025) to produce the first systematic application of modern AI evaluation standards to economic history research.
Current status. We have built the document infrastructure (approximately 97,000 pages of US fiscal documents extracted and structured) and demonstrated through a proof-of-concept that LLMs can identify fiscal policy language with high accuracy. We are now implementing a four-codebook architecture with systematic validation before deployment.
What we’re asking

API budget for development phase: ~$200–250 (codebook validation through early March)
API budget for production phase: ~$250–400 (US and Malaysia deployment, contingent on development results)
Identification of a Malaysia fiscal policy expert for validation (~25–40 hours)

The development phase produces a clear feasibility signal: if codebooks do not meet performance targets on US ground-truth data by early March, we will know the approach is not viable before committing to production costs.
Timeline

Full codebook evaluation suite validated: Early March 2026 (go/no-go decision point)
US benchmark deployment with ground-truth comparison: Late March 2026
Malaysia pilot deployment: April 2026
Full Southeast Asia dataset (Indonesia, Thailand, Philippines, Vietnam): June 2026
crumbs: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
Why This Matters
Governments change taxes and spending constantly, but most of these changes respond to current economic conditions. Stimulus during recessions and tax hikes during booms are endogenous to the business cycle. Using them to estimate fiscal multipliers produces biased results, because the policy change and the economic outcome are driven by the same underlying conditions.
Credible fiscal policy analysis requires isolating exogenous shocks: tax or spending changes that happened for reasons unrelated to current economic conditions (deficit reduction motivated by past decisions, or long-run structural reforms). Romer and Romer (2010) pioneered the “narrative approach” to identify these shocks by reading historical government documents and classifying the stated motivation for each fiscal action.
This has been done for the United States. For emerging markets, where fiscal policy decisions have enormous consequences for development outcomes, we have no comparable data.
Recent advances in Large Language Models may make it possible to scale this methodology for the first time. This project investigates whether LLMs, guided by carefully validated codebooks, can assist experts in identifying fiscal shocks across countries at a fraction of the traditional cost and time.
crumbs: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
Our Approach

Integrating Two Rigorous Frameworks
Our methodology synthesizes two established frameworks:

Romer and Romer (2010) (Fiscal Shock Identification): The gold-standard 6-phase methodology that defines what we measure: how to identify fiscal acts, classify their motivations, extract timing, and quantify magnitudes, taking original government documents into a fully identified dataset of fiscal shocks
Halterman and Keith (2025) (LLM Content Analysis): A 5-stage validation framework that defines how we ensure quality through systematic testing of LLM outputs before trusting them for research

This combination is the core methodological contribution: we apply modern AI evaluation standards to an established economic methodology, producing results that are both economically grounded and statistically validated.


Four Domain-Specific Codebooks
Each stage of the Romer & Romer methodology maps to a dedicated codebook with its own evaluation criteria:








Codebook Architecture


Each codebook handles one stage of the R&R methodology


Codebook
Task
Key Question
Replaces




C1: Measure Identification
Find fiscal measures in raw documents using the 'significant mention' rule
Is there a fiscal act here?
Model A


C2: Motivation Classification
Classify why each measure was enacted: deficit reduction, countercyclical, long-run growth, or spending
Is this shock exogenous to the business cycle?
Model B


C3: Timing Extraction1
Extract the quarter(s) when each measure takes effect
When does it take effect?
Model C (timing)


C4: Magnitude Estimation
Extract the fiscal impact (revenue or spending change) in billions of dollars
How large is it?
Model C (magnitude)



1 C3 and C4 were previously combined; separating them allows independent evaluation of each task.










Systematic Validation Before Deployment
Each codebook passes through five stages of testing before we use it on new countries. The key insight from Halterman and Keith (2025) is that LLM outputs must be validated before deployment, not after:








Five-Stage Validation Pipeline


Applied to each codebook before production deployment


Stage
What We Do
Why It Matters
Pass Criteria




S0: Codebook Design
Write precise definitions with examples; domain expert reviews
Garbage in, garbage out; clear instructions are the foundation
Expert approval of definitions and examples


S1: Competency Tests
Test whether the model follows instructions correctly and produces valid outputs
Catches fundamental failures before we invest in full evaluation
100% valid outputs; correctly handles all training examples; stable under reordering


S2: Performance Evaluation
Evaluate on all 44 labeled US fiscal acts, holding each out in turn
Measures real accuracy against ground truth with no data leakage
Meet target metrics per codebook (see below)


S3: Error Analysis
When the model fails, diagnose whether errors are random or systematic
Guides targeted improvements to codebook design
Documented failure patterns with improvement plan


S4: Model Retraining1
Retrain the model on our specific task (last resort)
Risks overfitting to US data, reducing ability to transfer to Malaysia
Only attempted if codebook improvements are exhausted



1 We actively avoid this stage. Retraining may cause the model to memorize US-specific patterns and fail on Malaysia.
crumbs: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
What Model A Taught Us
Our January proof-of-concept (Model A) demonstrated that LLMs understand fiscal policy language well enough to identify fiscal acts with 92% accuracy, 100% recall, and 86% precision. This validated the core premise of the project.
However, Model A classified pre-selected passages rather than extracting information from raw documents. This is a critical distinction:








Gap Between Proof-of-Concept and Production


What Model A Did
What Production Requires




Classify pre-selected passages
Extract relevant passages from raw documents


Binary yes/no on curated examples
Find fiscal acts across ~97,000 pages


US-specific prompt engineering
Cross-country transfer to Malaysia and Southeast Asia







The revised codebook architecture addresses each of these gaps directly. Model A confirmed that the LLM capability is there; the codebook framework ensures we can deploy it rigorously at scale.
crumbs: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
Success Criteria

What “Good Enough” Looks Like
Each codebook is evaluated against concrete targets:








Performance Targets by Codebook


Codebook
Requirement
Why This Threshold




C1: Measure ID
Find ≥90% of real fiscal acts
Missing real acts means incomplete dataset


C1: Measure ID
At least 80% of flagged items are real acts
Some false positives are acceptable; experts review candidates


C2: Motivation
Classify motivation correctly ≥70% of the time
Baseline for 4-category classification


C2: Motivation
When labeled exogenous, be right ≥85% of the time
Critical: only exogenous shocks are used for fiscal multiplier estimation


C3: Timing
Extract exact implementation quarter ≥85% of the time
Must match R&R implementation timing


C3: Timing
Within one quarter (3 months) ≥95% of the time
Acceptable tolerance for phased implementations


C4: Magnitude
Average magnitude error <30%
Must approximate R&R magnitude estimates


C4: Magnitude
Get direction right (increase vs. cut) ≥95% of the time
A tax increase coded as a cut would reverse the estimated effect









Cross-Country Transfer (The Key Test)
The central hypothesis is that codebooks validated on US data can transfer to Malaysia without retraining:

C1 (Measure Identification): Expert agreement ≥80%
C2 (Motivation Classification): Expert agreement ≥70%

Because Malaysia has no ground-truth labels, we rely on expert validation. If these thresholds are not met, we add Malaysia-specific examples to codebooks and re-evaluate, without retraining the base model.

Budget
API and expert costs are modest relative to the traditional alternative (6–12 months of full-time research assistant work per country). Once codebooks are validated, each additional country costs only ~$100–150 in API fees plus expert review hours, making regional scaling economically viable. Critically, the budget is structured in two phases: the development phase answers the feasibility question before production costs are incurred.

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
What We’ve Built So Far
The project has already delivered reusable infrastructure and validated the core approach:

Document corpus: Approximately 97,000 pages of US fiscal documents (Economic Reports of the President, Budget documents, Treasury Annual Reports) spanning 1946 to the present, extracted, structured, and ready for processing
Proof of concept: Model A demonstrated that LLMs identify fiscal policy language with 92% accuracy, confirming the feasibility of the approach
Methodological framework: A rigorous integration of R&R fiscal shock methodology with H&K LLM evaluation standards. This is the first application of this kind to economic history research
Validated codebook architecture: Four domain-specific codebooks (C1–C4) designed for systematic evaluation and cross-country transfer

The revised approach builds on this foundation. The document infrastructure and methodological framework are in place; what remains is codebook validation and deployment.

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
Our March 4 report concluded with the first of two classification steps approaching its performance targets. This report covers six weeks of development that produced two milestones.
The pipeline classifies fiscal policy changes in two steps. First, Measure Identification (C1) scans government documents to find passages that describe a fiscal policy change. Second, Motivation Classification (C2) determines why each measure was enacted—and, critically, whether it qualifies as an exogenous shock (a policy change not driven by current economic conditions). C2 itself has two sub-steps: evidence extraction (C2a) pulls motivation-relevant quotes from each passage, then act-level classification (C2b) synthesizes that evidence into a final judgment. Together, C1 and C2 produce the core deliverable: a set of fiscal policy changes identified as exogenous.
Milestone 1: C1 is validated and ready for production. C1 passed its error analysis gate (v0.6.0, iteration 28). Manual inspection of all predictions found zero semantic errors—every misclassification traced to ambiguity in how we defined the scope of “fiscal measure,” not to the model misunderstanding its instructions.
Milestone 2: C2 completed its first full evaluation cycle. The motivation classifier reached weighted F1 of 0.611 (target: 0.70) and exogenous precision of 0.789 (target: 0.85) on all 39 labeled acts. Manual error analysis revealed that the codebook, not the model, is the primary bottleneck: 10 of 15 errors trace to gaps in the classification instructions (category F), while only 3 are genuine model reasoning failures (category E). This is a positive finding—instruction gaps are addressable through revision.
The Halterman and Keith (2025) validation framework is working as designed. Each stage produced actionable diagnostics: behavioral tests (S1) confirmed instruction compliance, formal evaluation (S2) quantified performance, and error analysis (S3) identified specific revisions to close the gap. The same iterative process validated C1 in 28 iterations and is now driving C2 toward its targets.
crumbs: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
Classification Pipeline
Figure 1 shows the two-step classification architecture. C1 filters ~15,000 document passages down to those describing fiscal policy changes. C2 then determines why each measure was enacted: the evidence extraction step (C2a) pulls motivation-relevant quotes from each passage, and the classification step (C2b) synthesizes evidence across all passages for a given act into a single motivation judgment. The motivation category determines whether the act is exogenous (a shock suitable for causal analysis) or endogenous (a policy response to current conditions).

1 Deficit-driven and spending-driven are endogenous categories (not fiscal shocks). Long-run and countercyclical determine exogeneity.











Deficit-driven acts are classified near-perfectly (F1 = 0.842, 100% recall). The weakness is the boundary between long-run structural reforms and countercyclical stabilization: 7 of 15 long-run acts were misclassified as countercyclical. This is the dominant error pattern, and it directly affects exogeneity determination—long-run acts misclassified as countercyclical are incorrectly labeled endogenous.


Error Analysis: Classification Instructions are the Bottleneck
Manual analysis of all 39 acts using the Halterman and Keith (2025) six-category error framework revealed a favorable error distribution (Table 4).




Table 4: C2b Error Distribution (S3 Manual Analysis)












All 39 acts inspected collaboratively (human + AI)


Category
Count
Share
Implication




A: Model correct
24

0

E: Model semantic error1
3
7.7%

F: Codebook gap
10
25.6%
Codebook lacks guidance for these boundary cases



1 Category F errors are addressable through codebook revision. Category E errors may require model upgrade.











The key finding: 66.7% of errors (10/15) are category F — the codebook lacks guidance for specific boundary cases, particularly distinguishing structural reform from cyclical stabilization when both use similar language. Only 3 errors (20%) are genuine model reasoning failures. Two specific codebook gaps account for 7 of the 10 category F errors:

Return-to-normal test too literal. The classification instructions’ rule for distinguishing long-run from countercyclical takes policymaker rhetoric at face value. Romer and Romer (2010) read through rhetorical framing to underlying policy intent—a distinction the current instructions do not encode.
Frequency rule disadvantages long-run. The “most frequently cited motivation” decision rule systematically favors countercyclical because cyclical language fills more space in budget documents than structural intent statements.

Both gaps have clear revision paths. After revision, the realistic ceiling is approximately 95% accuracy (36–37 of 39 acts), with the remaining errors requiring additional congressional documents in the corpus.
crumbs: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs

title: Scaling Fiscal Shock Identification with LLMs
text:
Next Steps

C2 codebook revision (immediate): Sharpen the return-to-normal test and modify the frequency-based decision rule to weigh provision structure alongside stated framing. Rerun behavioral tests (S1) to confirm compliance, then formal evaluation (S2) and error analysis (S3) for updated metrics.
Model exploration (if codebook revision plateaus): The error analysis found that the model relies on label name semantics over definitions for boundary cases, suggesting Haiku may be too small for the multi-step reasoning these cases require. A larger model (Sonnet) is the fallback.
US production pilot (late April target): Once C2 passes its evaluation gate, run the validated C1+C2 pipeline on the full US corpus (~15,000 passages) to produce the first complete set of exogenous fiscal shock identifications for 1945–2022, and compare to Romer and Romer (2010) dataset.
South East Asia deployment (mid May): After validating the production machinery on the US, we can start analysing government documents from SEA to produce exogenous only fiscal shock datasets.
Timing and magnitude (future extension): Extracting when each measure takes effect and how large the fiscal impact is are natural extensions of this pipeline. These enrichments can be pursued if the World Bank office determines they are valuable, but the core deliverable—identifying which fiscal policy changes are exogenous—does not depend on them.
